title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 12 min

Welcome to the course, how it works, and what you’ll cover.

title: Introduction
body:
Welcome to your resource on UEECD0007 – Apply Work Health and Safety Regulations, Codes and Practices in the Workplace. Working in the electrotechnology industry involves dealing with electrical systems, high-risk environments and complex equipment. Understanding and applying work health and safety (WHS) regulations and practices is essential to protect yourself and others from harm while maintaining a safe and compliant work environment.

This resource is for electricians, electrical technicians, line workers, telecommunications technicians, automation technicians, renewable energy technicians and instrumentation technicians. It will help you develop the knowledge and skills needed to work safely in electrotechnology environments, identify hazards, implement risk controls and respond effectively to workplace incidents. By applying the procedures outlined in this resource, you’ll be able to manage risks and follow WHS laws and regulations confidently.

This Learner Guide will cover the following:

_graphic.alt: https://training.gov.au/UEECD0007

body:
• Preparing to enter an electrotechnology workplace
  • You will learn how to obtain work area access permits, follow relevant safety regulations and codes, apply safe work methods and prepare for isolation procedures.
  • Applying safe electrotechnology working practices
  • This chapter covers implementing risk control measures, responding to accidents and emergencies, applying safe work methods at heights and using proper manual handling techniques.
  • Following electrotechnology workplace procedures for hazard identification and risk control
  • You will learn how to identify and report hazards, complete WHS documentation and follow workplace instructions to meet legal and organisational requirements.

title: The Unit of Competency
body:
UEECD0007 - Apply work health and safety regulations, codes and practices in the workplace(Release 1)

This unit involves the skills and knowledge required to apply work health and safety (WHS)/occupational health and safety (OHS) regulations and codes of practices in the electrotechnology workplace.

It includes applying safe working practices, following workplace procedures for hazard identification and risk control. It also includes electrotechnology worker responsibilities and application for health and safety, risk management and adherence to safety practices as part of electrotechnology work functions when preparing to enter a work area.

  • Highlight keywords – Mark important terms in your Learner Guide to help remember critical concepts (do not mark borrowed books).
  • Engage in discussions – Consult and collaborate with your peers, fellow students or trainer.
  • Assess your learning style – Determine whether you are an auditory, visual or kinaesthetic learner and adjust your study methods to suit your style.
  • Relate to personal experiences – Connect new information to your own experiences and existing knowledge to make it more relatable and easier to grasp.

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 8 min

You will learn about different types of work area access permits, when they are required and how to apply for them.

title: Obtain Work Area Access Permits
body:
A work area access permit is a formal authorisation document allowing a person to enter and work in specific areas with safety risks. In electrotechnology workplaces, these permits help to:

Ensure all safety checks are completed before starting work to identify hazards and control risks

Ensure only trained and authorised workers enter hazardous areas

Stop unauthorised entry into restricted areas, reducing the risk of accidents

Provide clear information about potential hazards, such as live electrical parts, confined spaces and hazardous gases, before work begins

Ensure compliance with workplace safety rules and procedures, including work health and safety (WHS) regulations, codes of practice and safe work method statements (SWMS)

Types of Work Area Access Permits

Different types of work area access permits may be required based on the specific risks and tasks performed. Here are some common types of work area access permits for electrical work:

TypeWhen is it required?What is included in the permit?
Confined space entry permitWhen working in small or enclosed spaces like electrical vaults, underground cable pits or switchgear roomsAtmospheric testing Ventilation requirements Emergency rescue plan PPE
Electrical work permitBefore working on electrical systems, especially when there is a risk of electric shock, arc flash or contact with live partsSafety rules for isolation procedures PPE Testing for de-energisation Risk assessments

TypeWhen is it required?What is included in the permit?
High Voltage (HV) Work PermitWhen working on high-voltage electrical systems like substations, transformers and overhead power linesSafe isolation procedures Lockout/tagout (LOTO) PPE Risk control measures Emergency response plans
Hot Work PermitFor any task that creates heat, sparks or flames, such as welding, soldering, cutting or grindingFire safety controls Fire watch personnel Fire extinguisher availability PPE Ventilation requirements
Isolation PermitWhen shutting down power to electrical equipment before maintenance, repairs or installationLockout/tagout (LOTO) procedures Electrical testing Verification of de-energisation PPE Hazard assessment
Work at Height PermitWhen working at heights of 2 meters (6.5 feet) or more, such as on ladders, scaffolding, rooftops or elevated work platforms (EWPs)Fall prevention measures Risk assessments Equipment checks Emergency rescue plans

Work area access permits are issued by authorised personnel who oversee safety and compliance. These may include:

Electrical supervisor – Checks that all electrical safety procedures are followed before approving work in electrical zones

Safety officer/ WHS coordinator – Makes sure the workplace follows safety rules, checks for hazards and confirms risk assessments are done

_graphic.alt: Identified hazards and control measures (e.g. PPE, lockout/tagout, fir

body:
Site or project manager – Gives permits for construction, maintenance and installation work on electrical systems in places like factories, offices and homes

Control room operator/ facility manager – Manages access to electrical areas in power plants, substations and industrial facilities

Following workplace procedures for obtaining a work area access permit ensures safety, efficiency and regulatory compliance. These procedures provide a structured approach to identifying hazards, verifying risk controls and ensuring that only authorised personnel enter potentially dangerous work areas.

The following are general steps for obtaining a work area access permit:

Identify the need for a permit. Workers can determine if a work area access permit is required by:

Checking workplace safety policies – Review WHS policies, permit-to-work systems or safety handbooks for permit requirements.

Reviewing SWMS and JSA – Check the following documents to determine if the task requires a permit based on the type of work, associated risks and necessary safety controls:

Safe Work Method Statement (SWMS) – This is a document that outlines high-risk work activities, potential hazards and control measures to ensure safety.

Job Safety Analysis (JSA) – This is a step-by-step breakdown of a task, identifying hazards at each stage and how to control them.

Identifying high-risk work – You can quickly assess the task by asking:

‘Am I working near exposed electrical wires or live equipment?’

‘Do I need to enter a tight or enclosed space?’

‘Will my task create sparks, heat or flames?’

‘Am I using a ladder, scaffold or elevated work platform (EWP) above 2 meters?’

Confirming with authorised personnel – If unsure, consult the supervisor, safety officer or site manager to verify permit requirements.

Complete the permit application. Fill out the permit form, including:

Work details (task type, location, duration)

Identified hazards and control measures (e.g. PPE, lockout/tagout, fire safety)

_graphic.alt: Once approved, the permit must be displayed at the worksite or carried

body:
If required, attach supporting documents such as:

SWMS (mandatory for high-risk work)

JSA (if workplace policy requires it)

Risk assessment forms (if additional hazard controls are needed)

Submit the permit for review and approval.

The permit application is submitted before work begins to the authorised personnel (e.g. site supervisor, safety officer, electrical manager).

The review process ensures that all risk controls are in place. If the task involves high-risk work, a pre-work safety briefing or toolbox talk may be required before approval.

Review timelines vary by workplace and permit type:

Routine work permits (e.g. standard electrical maintenance) – Approved on the same day or within 24 hours.

High-risk work permits (e.g. high-voltage access, confined space entry) – May require 2–5 days for approval, depending on risk assessments and required sign-offs.

Some organisations require multiple levels of approval (e.g. safety officer and manager for high-risk work). Others allow site supervisors to approve permits directly.

If the application meets all safety requirements, the authorised personnel sign and approve the permit—at this point, the document becomes the official work authorisation.

Receive and display the permit.

Once approved, the permit must be displayed at the worksite or carried by the worker as per workplace procedures. Some workplaces require physical copies displayed at the worksite. Others use digital permit systems for real-time tracking and approvals.

If there are any changes to the work scope or conditions, the permit may need to be reassessed or reissued.

Close the permit after completing the task.

After finishing the work, the worker must report back to the authorised person.

A final safety check is performed to ensure the worksite is safe before officially closing the permit.

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 11 min

This section explains key WHS laws, regulations and codes of practice that apply to electrical work and how to comply with them.

title: Follow Relevant Safety Regulations and Codes
body:
Electricians often work in environments where high-voltage systems, electrical hazards, heights and confined spaces pose significant risks. Work health and safety (WHS) laws, regulations and codes of practice exist to reduce workplace risks, prevent injuries and ensure everyone goes home safely at the end of the day.

WHS Principles

WHS laws and guidelines are built on key principles that ensure a safe working environment. These include:
displayTitle: Follow Relevant Safety Regulations and Codes

title: 1.2.1 WHS Legislation
body:
WHS legislation is designed to protect workers and others from harm by setting clear legal requirements for workplace safety.

WHS laws are guided by both aims and objectives:

  • An aim is the broad, long-term purpose behind the law — it explains what the law is trying to achieve overall.
  • An objective is a clear, specific action or result that helps achieve that aim or a bigger purpose.

The aim or purpose of WHS laws includes:

  • Prevent workplace injuries and illnesses by reducing or removing risks as much as possible.
  • Create clear and consistent safety rules that apply to all industries and workplaces.
  • Encourage a culture of safety where employers and workers take responsibility for keeping the workplace safe.
  • Ensure fair and open discussions about workplace safety so that everyone has a say in making improvements.
WHS laws are different depending on the state or territory you work in:

State/territoryWHS law
Australian Capital Territory, New South Wales, Northern Territory, Queensland, South Australia, TasmaniaFollow their own versions of the Work Health and Safety Act 2011
VictoriaFollows the Occupational Health and Safety Act 2004
Western AustraliaBased on the Model WHS Act but tailored for WA industries — including electrical work

In electrotechnology workplaces, the objectives of the WHS Act 2011 help ensure that electrical tasks are done safely and legally. These include:

  • Requiring businesses to identify electrical hazards and control the risks
  • Ensuring workers can raise safety concerns about electrical tasks or equipment
  • Promoting teamwork and consultation between electrical workers and management to improve safety
  • Providing training and instructions so electrical workers understand risks like arc flash, electric shock or unsafe isolation
  • Enforcing rules to make sure only competent and authorised workers perform electrical tasks
  • Keeping safety standards consistent across states and industries, including those working on electrical installations and repairs
  • Supporting regular reviews of safety procedures, such as lockout/tagout systems or PPE use

Victoria’s health and safety law is different but aims for the same outcomes. For electrotechnology workers, the OHS Act 2004 focuses on:

  • Making employers and employees directly responsible for electrical safety
  • Requiring employers to provide safe electrical tools, systems of work and procedures (e.g. testing equipment before use)
  • Ensuring workers receive proper supervision and training, including safe isolation procedures
  • Using Victorian Compliance Codes to guide electrical safety practices (similar to Codes of Practice in other states)

Western Australia’s WHS Act 2020 applies across general industries, mining and petroleum — and includes special rules for electrical work. Objectives include:

  • Integrating electrical safety standards across industries
  • Requiring Residual Current Devices (RCDs) to be installed and maintained to protect workers from shock
  • Mandating test and tag procedures for portable electrical equipment in workplaces
  • Requiring licensing for high-risk electrical work, such as live testing or working on switchboards

No matter which law applies in your state or territory, the goal is the same: to make electrical work safe. That means:

Health and Safety Committees and Representatives

Keeping a workplace safe is not just the responsibility of employers—workers also help in making sure risks are identified and addressed. To help with this, workplaces can have Health and Safety Committees (HSCs) and Health and Safety Representatives (HSRs). These groups and individuals give workers a say in safety decisions and help make sure that everyone follows the rules to prevent injuries and hazards.

A Health and Safety Committee (HSC) is a team made up of both workers and employers. Their job is to work together to improve safety and make sure the workplace follows health and safety laws.

According to the Work Health and Safety Act 2011 (Part 5, Division 4), an HSC:

HSCs meet at least once every three months or whenever at least half of its members request a meeting. These meetings allow everyone to share their concerns and find solutions together.

A Health and Safety Representative (HSR) is a worker who is elected by their team to speak up about safety issues. Their job is to make sure workers' concerns are heard and that safety measures are properly followed.

Under the Work Health and Safety Act 2011 (Part 5, Division 3), an HSR can:

To do their job well, HSRs must receive proper training and be included in discussions about safety. They do not enforce laws, but they play a big role in making sure employers follow the rules and that workers stay safe.

Health and Safety Inspectors

A health and safety inspector is an appointed officer who enforces WHS laws in the workplace. Inspectors work for the state or territory WHS regulator, such as SafeWork NSW or WorkSafe Victoria. Their job is to ensure that businesses and workers follow WHS laws and that workplaces are safe for everyone.

Inspectors are officially appointed under the Work Health and Safety Act 2011 (in states and territories where it applies, such as NSW and QLD) and under equivalent legislation in other jurisdictions (e.g. the OHS Act 2004 in Victoria).

Under Part 9, Division 2 of the WHS Act, inspectors have a range of powers to enforce workplace safety laws. These include:

  • Inspecting workplaces – Inspectors can visit worksites, check equipment and review safety procedures to ensure businesses comply with WHS laws.
  • Issuing improvement notices – If a workplace is not meeting WHS requirements, inspectors can issue an improvement notice. This notice tells the employer what needs to be fixed and sets a deadline for making the necessary changes.
  • Issuing prohibition notices – If there is an immediate risk to worker safety, inspectors can issue a prohibition notice. This stops the work immediately until the hazard is fixed.
  • Investigating incidents – Inspectors investigate workplace accidents, near misses and complaints to find out what went wrong and how similar incidents can be prevented.
  • Questioning workers and employers – Inspectors have the right to ask questions, interview workers and request documents related to WHS. Employers and workers must cooperate and provide the necessary information.
  • Entering workplaces without notice – Inspectors can enter a workplace at any time without prior notice if they believe there is a WHS concern.
  • Taking evidence – If necessary, inspectors can take photos, samples and records as part of their investigation.

Responsibilities, Rights and Obligations

Under WHS laws, both employers (also called PCBUs — Persons Conducting a Business or Undertaking) and employees (workers) have legal obligations, responsibilities and rights to ensure a safe and healthy workplace.

You learnt earlier that the WHS Act 2011 applies in most parts of Australia. This includes New South Wales, Queensland, South Australia, Tasmania, the Northern Territory and the Australian Capital Territory. However, Victoria uses the OHS Act 2004 and Western Australia uses the WHS Act 2020.

While each Act shares the same goals of reducing workplace harm and promoting safe practices, the terminology and legal structures may differ slightly. For example, the WHS Acts use the term ‘PCBU’, while Victoria continues to use ‘employer’.

These elements outline what is expected, required and protected under WHS laws. The following are the definitions of each element:

  • Obligations – Legal requirements that must be met under the WHS Act. Failure to comply can lead to penalties.
  • Responsibilities – Ongoing duties that PCBUs and workers should follow to maintain workplace health and safety.
  • Rights – Protections and entitlements that allow workers to participate in WHS matters and refuse unsafe work.
The table below provides examples of these obligations, responsibilities and rights.

EmployerEmployee
Obligations (Legal requirements)Eliminate or minimise risks as much as reasonably practicable. Provide safe equipment and tools for electrical work. Ensure that workers follow safety procedures before performing high-risk tasks. Maintain records of WHS incidents, risk assessments and control measures. Failure to meet these legal obligations can result in fines or legal action.Follow all reasonable safety instructions given by the PCBU or supervisor. Use PPE and safety equipment as required. Do not put themselves or others at risk through unsafe work practices. Workers who fail to meet these obligations may face disciplinary action or be legally held responsible for workplace safety breaches.
Responsibilities (Ongoing duties)Provide and maintain a safe working environment. Ensure workers have access to training, supervision and safety procedures. Provide safe work systems for electrical tasks, including lockout/tagout procedures. Consult workers on health and safety matters.Take reasonable care of their own health and safety. Follow workplace safety procedures and use PPE correctly. Report hazards and unsafe conditions to their supervisor. Cooperate with WHS policies and participate in safety training.
Rights (Entitlements)The right to enforce WHS procedures and discipline workers who fail to follow safety rules The right to conduct risk assessments before work begins The right to refuse work from contractors or workers who do not follow WHS standardsThe right to a safe workplace – Employers must take all reasonable steps to ensure worker safety. The right to be consulted on WHS matters – Workers should have a say in safety policies and procedures. The right to refuse unsafe work – If a worker believes a task is unsafe (e.g. exposed live electrical wiring), they can stop work and report it. The right to report WHS concerns without retaliation – Employers cannot punish a worker for raising safety concerns.
You can access the full Commonwealth WHS Act and corresponding legislation in each state/territory in the table below:

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 2 min

How regulations turn the law into enforceable safety requirements.

title: 1.2.2 WHS Regulation
body:
While WHS legislation outlines the general legal framework for workplace health and safety, WHS regulations provide detailed and enforceable requirements on how to manage specific risks and implement safety measures. These regulations ensure that duty holders, including PCBUs, supervisors and workers, follow clear safety standards to reduce workplace hazards.

In the electrotechnology industry, WHS regulations establish specific safety requirements for electrical work. Some of the most relevant provisions include:

  • Managing electrical risks – Requires workplaces to identify electrical hazards, implement risk controls and ensure proper lockout/tagout (LOTO) procedures before working on electrical systems.
  • Safe work procedures – Specifies that high-risk electrical tasks, such as working on or near live equipment, must follow Safe Work Method Statements (SWMS) and risk assessment processes.
  • Personal protective equipment (PPE) – Mandates that workers wear appropriate PPE, such as insulated gloves, arc-rated clothing and face shields, when working with electrical hazards.
  • Training and competency – Requires electricians and workers performing electrical tasks to be competent and properly trained before handling live circuits, high-voltage systems or hazardous energy sources.
  • Working at heights – Provides safety standards for installing electrical systems at elevated locations, including the use of harnesses, scaffolding and fall protection.
  • Emergency procedures – Requires workplaces to have clear protocols for electrical emergencies, including electric shock response, first aid and incident reporting.
The table below provides links to the complete Commonwealth and state/territory WHS regulations:

State/territoryRegulation

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 9 min

Practical guidance for meeting your WHS duties.

title: 1.2.3 Codes of Practice
body:
SafeWork Australia develops codes of practice to provide practical guidance on complying with WHS laws. These documents bridge the gap between regulations and real-world safety practices, helping workers and businesses meet their legal obligations while maintaining a safe work environment.

For electricians, several codes of practice are particularly relevant, covering areas such as electrical risks, safe work procedures, working at heights and emergency response.

The table below outlines key codes of practice that apply to electrical work and safety in the electrotechnology industry.

Code of practiceBrief overviewPractices relevant to electricians
Managing electrical risks in the workplaceProvides guidance on identifying, assessing and controlling electrical hazards in the workplaceEnsure safe isolation procedures before working on electrical equipment. Use lockout/tagout (LOTO) systems to prevent accidental re-energisation. Work safely near live parts or de-energised circuits. Understand arc flash risks and protection measures.
Managing the risk of falls at workplacesProvides guidance on preventing fall-related injuries, particularly for workers at heightsUse ladders, scaffolds or elevated work platforms (EWPs) safely. Ensure fall protection systems (harnesses, guardrails) are in place when working on high electrical installations. Implement safe work procedures for rooftop electrical work.
Construction workCovers WHS requirements for construction sites, including electrical installations, maintenance and commissioningFollow permits and site safety procedures before electrical work Understand high-risk construction work requirements for electricians Ensure electrical systems comply with construction site WHS rules
First aid in the workplaceProvides guidance on first aid procedures, training and equipment for workplace safetyKnow first aid procedures for electrical shock and burns. Know the location of first-aid kits in the workplace. Train in CPR (cardiopulmonary resuscitation) and emergency response for electrical incidents.

Code of practiceBrief overviewPractices relevant to electricians
Hazardous Manual TasksFocuses on preventing musculoskeletal injuries caused by lifting, pulling, pushing or repetitive movementsLift and carry heavy electrical components safely. Use ergonomic work techniques when handling cables and tools. Avoid awkward postures when working in confined spaces.
Managing Risks of Hazardous Chemicals in the WorkplaceProvides guidance on safely handling, storing and using hazardous chemicals to prevent exposure and injury.Identify hazardous chemicals used in electrical work (e.g. contact cleaners, insulating varnishes). Follow correct storage and labelling requirements for hazardous chemicals. Use appropriate PPE when handling chemicals (e.g. gloves, respirators). Ensure ventilation when working with fumes from electrical components.

title: Further reading
body: For more detailed guidance on safe work practices and compliance requirements, you can refer to the Codes of Practice provided by Safe Work Australia. Codes of Practice https://www.safeworkaustralia.gov.au/law-and-regulation/codes-practice

WHS legislation, regulations and codes of practice define the responsibilities of different duty holders in maintaining workplace health and safety. A duty holder is anyone with legal responsibilities to ensure health and safety in the workplace. Different duty holders have different responsibilities, depending on the framework that applies.

The WHS Act outlines broad legal duties for each duty holder.

WHS Regulations provide specific, enforceable requirements related to high-risk work, training and workplace controls.

_graphic.alt: Ensure ventilation when working with fumes from electrical components.

body:
Codes of Practice offer practical guidance on how duty holders can meet their legal responsibilities.

The table below breaks down how these responsibilities apply to key duty holders:

Duty holderResponsibilities under WHS legislationResponsibilities under WHS regulationsResponsibilities under codes of practice
Person conducting a business or undertaking (PCBU) Examples: electrical contractor, electrical installation company, maintenance service providerHas the primary duty of care to ensure workplace safety Must eliminate or minimise risks as far as reasonably practicable Must provide safe work systems, equipment and PPEMust ensure risk assessments, permits and safe isolation procedures are in place before electrical work Must provide specific training and supervision for high-risk electrical tasks Must follow licensing and competency requirements for electrical workShould follow industry best practices Should provide clear safety instructions to workers based on risk level Should consult codes of practice for guidance on hazard management

Duty holderResponsibilities under WHS legislationResponsibilities under WHS regulationsResponsibilities under codes of practice
Officers Examples: electrical site supervisor, project manager, workshop manager, WHS coordinatorMust exercise due diligence to ensure the PCBU complies with WHS laws Must actively monitor workplace safety and risk controls Must ensure workers receive WHS training and supervisionMust implement safety control measures before electrical work Must ensure workers are trained in electrical isolation proceduresShould follow recommended safety practices in managing electrical risks in the workplace Should conduct pre-work briefings and toolbox talks before high-risk work
Workers Examples: electrical technician, apprentice, subcontractor, line worker, automation technicianMust take reasonable care of their own safety and that of others Must follow WHS procedures, PPE requirements and safety instructions Must report hazards and unsafe practicesMust comply with safe work procedures and electrical safety regulations Must not perform electrical work without proper training, certification or supervision Must use PPE correctlyShould follow best practices for working on live electrical parts, as recommended in codes of practice Should refer to the Managing Electrical Risks in the Workplace Code of Practice for safe procedures
Other persons Examples: building owner, facility manager, delivery driver, non-electrical contractorMust follow workplace safety rules and instructions Must not interfere with electrical safety measures or proceduresMust follow entry permit and safety requirements before accessing restricted areas Must wear PPE if required in electrical zonesShould be aware of potential electrical hazards and follow all safety signage and barriers

In the workplace, identifying relevant requirements and guidelines in WHS regulations and codes of practice is a practical process that ensures workers comply with safety requirements before entering an electrotechnology work area. This is typically done through the following methods:

Reviewing workplace safety procedures – Workers must check their company's electrical safety procedures, permit-to-work systems and lockout/tagout (LOTO) protocols to determine which WHS regulations and codes apply before starting a job.

Referring to WHS regulations and codes of practice – Workers should refer to SafeWork Australia's codes of practice and relevant WHS regulations to understand required safety measures, including isolation procedures and safe work distances from live equipment.

_graphic.alt: Referring to WHS regulations and codes of practice – Workers should re

body:
Consulting with authorised personnel – Before entering a high-risk electrical area, workers should consult with supervisors, electrical engineers or safety officers to confirm which safety rules apply. For example, a high-voltage work permit may be required before accessing substations or power distribution systems.

Participating in electrical safety inductions and training – Before working on a new site, workers undergo site-specific inductions covering key WHS regulations, safe work procedures and emergency response protocols.

Using WHS signage and worksite safety documents – Work areas often display electrical hazard signage, arc flash boundaries and isolation procedure instructions. These help workers quickly identify and follow the correct safety regulations.

Attending pre-work briefings or toolbox talks – Daily toolbox talks or pre-start meetings reinforce WHS regulations and safe work practices specific to electrical tasks, such as testing for dead before working on circuits or ensuring safe approach distances from overhead power lines.

The purpose of identifying relevant WHS regulations and codes of practice is to ensure that workers understand and apply the correct safety measures in their daily work. Following these requirements maintains a safe and compliant electrotechnology workplace. Workers apply these safety requirements in the following ways:

Adhering to safe work procedures – Workers must follow workplace policies and procedures aligned with WHS laws, such as safe isolation procedures, lockout/tagout (LOTO) protocols and arc flash protection measures before working on electrical systems.

Using required PPE and safety equipment – Workers must wear appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE), including insulated gloves, arc-rated clothing, face shields and dielectric boots, as required by WHS regulations for electrical work.

Following permit-to-work systems – Before working in hazardous areas (e.g. confined spaces, high-voltage switchyards), workers must obtain and comply with work permits, ensuring risk controls are in place before starting the job.

Applying risk control measures – WHS codes of practice require workers to follow risk assessment procedures, such as testing for dead before working on de-energised equipment, maintaining safe approach distances from live electrical parts and using residual current devices (RCDs) where necessary.

Participating in WHS consultation and training – Workers must attend safety meetings, toolbox talks and refresher training on new WHS regulations, ensuring ongoing compliance and awareness of industry best practices.

Reporting hazards and incidents – Under WHS laws, workers are required to immediately report electrical hazards, near misses and incidents to supervisors or safety officers, ensuring corrective actions are taken to prevent further risks.

Maintaining compliance with WHS documentation – Workers record risk assessments, equipment inspections and safety checklists in accordance with WHS requirements, ensuring compliance with workplace policies and legal obligations.

_graphic.alt: Maintaining compliance with WHS documentation – Workers record risk as

⏱ About 3 min

The Australian standards that apply to electrical work.

title: 1.2.4 Industry Standards
body:
Industry standards ensure that work is performed safely and consistently. The most relevant industry standards for electrical work are Australian/New Zealand Standards (AS/NZS). These standards provide technical specifications, installation methods and safety procedures. These are used alongside WHS laws and codes of practice to maintain compliance and best practices in the workplace.

Below are the key standards relevant to electrical work. These standards provide essential guidance for daily workplace procedures, ensuring safety, compliance and best practices in electrical installations, maintenance and testing.

AS/NZS StandardsOverviewApplication to workplace procedures
AS/NZS 3000:2018 (Wiring Rules)Defines minimum safety requirements for electrical installations, covering design, selection, installation and verificationFollow correct wiring setups to ensure electrical systems work safely. Keep enough space around electrical equipment to prevent short circuits and overheating. Make sure wires and equipment are properly connected to the ground to avoid electric shocks.
AS/NZS 4836:2011 (Safe Working on Low Voltage Electrical Installations)Provides safe work procedures for electricians working on live and de-energised low-voltage systemsUse safety locks and tags to make sure power is completely off before working. Always check that power is truly off before starting work on circuits. Wear protective gloves and use special insulated tools to stay safe from electric shocks.
AS/NZS 3017:2007 (Electrical Installations – Verification Guidelines)Outlines verification and testing procedures to confirm electrical installations meet compliance standardsTest electrical systems to ensure they are working properly before turning them on. Check that all electrical setups follow safety rules before completing the work.

AS/NZS StandardsOverviewApplication to workplace procedures
AS/NZS 3760:2022 (In-service Safety Inspection and Testing of Electrical Equipment)Specifies test and tag procedures for inspecting electrical equipment in workplacesRegularly inspect power tools and equipment to ensure they are safe to use. Look for damaged wires, faulty plugs or any signs of wear and tear before using equipment. Keep records of safety checks and test results to show compliance with safety
AS/NZS 4509.1:2009 (Stand-alone Power Systems – Safety Requirements)Provides guidelines for installing and maintaining stand-alone power systems, such as solar and battery storageEnsure solar panels and battery systems are properly connected and safe to use. Follow safe methods to store and handle batteries to prevent overheating or leaks. Check that voltage levels and circuit protection meet safety standards before installation.

title: Further reading
body: For more detailed information on Australian standards related to electrical work and safety, you can refer to the resources available from Standards Australia. Standards Australia https://www.standards.org.au/

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 6 min

You will learn how to follow safe work methods to control risks and complete electrical tasks safely.

title: Apply Safe Work Methods for Risk Control
body:
Safe work methods are structured procedures and best practices that outline the correct way to perform tasks in a safe, efficient and compliant manner.

Following safe work methods for risk control before starting a task helps workers:

Recognise potential hazards (e.g. live electrical components, confined spaces, high-voltage equipment)
displayTitle: Apply Safe Work Methods for Risk Control

body:
Implement risk control measures (e.g. using personal protective equipment, following isolation procedures)

Comply with WHS regulations and workplace safety requirements

Documents Containing Safe Work Methods

Safe work methods are documented in various workplace safety resources that outline step-by-step procedures for completing tasks safely. Workers can access these methods in the following documents:

Safe Work Method Statements (SWMS) – These are detailed documents outlining high-risk work activities, associated hazards and control measures. These are required for tasks such as working near live electrical parts, confined spaces or at heights.

Job Safety Analysis (JSA) – This document contains a breakdown of each task into steps, identifying hazards and risk controls at every stage. JSAs help workers understand specific dangers and the precautions needed before starting work.

_graphic.alt: Manufacturer’s instructions – These are manuals provided by equipment

body:
Risk assessments – These assessments evaluate potential hazards and the effectiveness of existing control measures. They ensure that workers are aware of risks and follow necessary safety protocols before proceeding with a task.

Standard operating procedures (SOPs) – These are workplace-specific guidelines detailing how tasks should be performed in compliance with WHS regulations and industry standards. SOPs often include instructions for using tools, handling electrical equipment and conducting tests safely.

Permits to work – These are authorised documents required for high-risk tasks, such as electrical isolation, working in hazardous areas or performing maintenance on live equipment. These permits ensure all safety requirements are met before work begins.

Manufacturer’s instructions – These are manuals provided by equipment manufacturers that outline correct operation, maintenance and safety precautions for electrical tools and systems. Following these instructions helps prevent misuse and accidents.

Workers can obtain these documents from:

Workplace safety management systems – If the documents are online, workers should check the company’s safety database before starting work. If printed copies are used, they should check the noticeboard or safety manual section in their work area.

Supervisors and WHS officers – Workers should check with their supervisor or WHS officer if they need a specific safety document for their job. In some cases, they may be required to review and sign the document before work begins.

Pre-work safety meetings or toolbox talks – Workers should attend these short meetings before starting work. During this meeting, they may receive:

Verbal or written safety instructions – A summary of hazards and how to control them

_graphic.alt: Supervisor’s approval on a permit-to-work, signed SWMS (Safe Work Meth

body:
Permit and documentation requirements – Information on whether a work area access permit, SWMS or risk assessment is needed.

When reading the documents containing safe work methods, workers should focus on information that helps identify risks and apply effective control measures before starting any task. The table below outlines key details to focus on:

DetailDescriptionExample
Task description or work activityThe specific task being performed, including steps involvedInstalling a switchboard, repairing faulty wiring, testing electrical circuits
Hazards identifiedPotential dangers that could cause harm if not managed properlyExposed live wires, arc flash risk, confined spaces in electrical panels
Risk control measuresSafety measures to reduce or eliminate risksUsing insulated tools, following lockout/tagout (LOTO) procedures, wearing PPE
Personal protective equipment (PPE)Protective equipment needed to safely perform the taskInsulated gloves, arc-rated face shield, rubber-soled boots
Emergency proceduresActions to take in case of an incident or hazard exposureSteps for responding to electric shock, fire safety protocols, first aid procedures
Authorisation and sign-offConfirmation that the necessary safety checks and authorisations are completed before work startsSupervisor’s approval on a permit-to-work, signed SWMS (Safe Work Method Statement)

Applying Safe Work Methods for Risk Control

Applying safe work methods is essential before starting any electrical work. Workers must make sure all risk control measures are in place to comply with WHS procedures and prevent accidents. Following a structured approach helps reduce risks such as electric shock, arc flash and equipment failure.

  • Review safe work method documents. Before starting work, workers should review safety documents to understand hazards and the required controls. Workers must:
  • Check SWMS and JSA to identify risks and safety measures for the task.
  • Review permit-to-work requirements if working in high-risk areas (e.g. confined spaces, high-voltage zones).
  • Understand emergency procedures in case something goes wrong, such as an electric shock incident.
  • Inspect the work area for hazards. After reviewing safety documents, workers must check the worksite for any potential dangers. For example:
  • Look for exposed wires, damaged insulation or moisture near electrical panels.
  • Ensure required permits and additional safety controls (e.g. barriers, warning signs) are in place.
  • Confirm emergency exits and fire safety equipment are accessible.
  • Implement risk control measures. Before beginning any electrical task, apply the required safety measures to minimise risks. For example:
  • De-energise equipment and test for dead before working.
  • Secure circuit breakers and disconnect switches to prevent accidental re-energisation.
  • Use insulated gloves, arc-rated clothing and rubber-soled boots for protection.
  • Ensure compliance with workplace safety policies. Following workplace procedures helps maintain safety and legal compliance. Workers must
  • Ensure all required documents are signed off (e.g. SWMS, risk assessments, permits).
  • Participate in a toolbox talk or pre-work briefing to go over safety procedures as required.
  • Follow permit-to-work conditions (e.g. ensure safety barriers, exclusion zones and warning signs are set up as needed).
  • Report and communicate hazards. If new risks appear, workers must report and address them before continuing. They must also communicate with team members to ensure everyone is aware of safety measures and hazards.

_graphic.alt: Report and communicate hazards. If new risks appear, workers must repo

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 15 min

This section covers how to isolate electrical and non-electrical systems to prevent accidental activation during maintenance and repairs.

title: Prepare for Electrical and Non-Electrical Isolation
body: • Isolation means making sure that electrical or mechanical systems are separated from their power sources so they cannot start unexpectedly while work is being carried out. This helps to prevent accidents and injuries.
displayTitle: Prepare for Electrical and Non-Electrical Isolation

_graphic.alt: Prepare for Electrical and Non-Electrical Isolation

body:
Electrical isolation refers to disconnecting electrical circuits or equipment from their power source to prevent accidental energisation or unexpected power activation. Examples of electrical isolation include:

  • Switching off and locking out a circuit breaker before working on an electrical panel.
  • Using a voltage tester to confirm a circuit is de-energised before repairs.
  • Applying lockout/tagout (LOTO) devices on electrical switchgear to prevent accidental re-energization.
  • Isolating and testing high-voltage equipment before performing maintenance in a substation.
  • Disconnecting power to an electric motor before servicing its internal components.

_graphic.alt: Non-electrical isolation refers to shutting off and securing energy so

body:
Proper electrical isolation is essential in electrotechnology workplaces to:

  • Keep workers safe – Prevents electric shocks, burns and other serious injuries by making sure no one accidentally touches live electrical parts
  • Prevent accidents – Stops machines from turning on unexpectedly, reduces the risk of electrical fires and prevents dangerous arc flashes (sudden electrical discharges)
  • Protect equipment – Prevents damage to electrical circuits, machines and tools by ensuring power is fully turned off before repairs or maintenance
  • Follow WHS safety rules – Helps workers and businesses meet legal safety requirements, including lockout/tagout (LOTO) procedures to prevent accidental power restoration
  • Make repairs safer – Ensures that workers can fix, upgrade or test electrical systems without the risk of unexpected voltage or sudden power surges
  • Keep safety systems working – Ensures that emergency stops, alarms and safety locks function properly without interference from electrical power

_graphic.alt: Description

body:
Non-electrical isolation refers to shutting off and securing energy sources that are not electrical, such as gas, steam, hydraulic, pneumatic or mechanical systems. It ensures that machines, pressure systems or moving parts do not unexpectedly activate while work is in progress. Examples of non-electrical isolation include:

  • Closing and locking a main gas valve before repairing a gas pipeline
  • Releasing stored pressure in hydraulic machinery before replacing hoses or fittings
  • Draining hazardous chemicals from a tank before performing maintenance on a storage system
  • Restricting access to an area where high-pressure steam pipes are being repaired
  • Securing mechanical moving parts with safety blocks before repairing a conveyor belt
  • Proper isolation of energy sources—whether electrical, mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic or chemical—maintains a safe work environment. Failing to follow correct isolation procedures can lead to serious hazards. One major risk is the loss of control over machines, systems or processes, which can result in severe injuries, equipment damage or hazardous spills.
  • The table below outlines how these risks can occur in the workplace due to inadequate isolation procedures.

_graphic.alt: Commonly used for electrical switchboards, machine control panels, cir

body:
RisksDescriptionExample
Loss of machine controlMachines and equipment that rely on energy sources (e.g. electricity, hydraulic pressure or compressed air) can suddenly start up if they are not properly isolated before maintenance or repairs. This can cause serious injuries to workers or damage to equipment.A technician is repairing a hydraulic press but does not fully release stored pressure. When another worker activates the controls, the press suddenly moves, crushing the technician’s hand.
Loss of system controlMany industrial systems rely on interconnected energy sources. Failing to fully isolate one part of a system could still leave other components active, increasing the risk of electric shocks, pressurised releases or mechanical malfunctions.A worker is replacing a damaged circuit breaker but does not shut off power from all sources. A secondary power supply keeps part of the switchboard energised, leading to accidental contact with live components.
Loss of process controlSome workplace tasks follow an automated sequence, such as manufacturing, chemical processing or power distribution. If isolation is not done correctly, these processes can restart unexpectedly, creating hazardous conditions.A maintenance worker is servicing a gas pipeline. Because the supply valve was not properly closed, toxic fumes start leaking, creating an immediate health risk.

_graphic.alt: Used for faulty electrical panels, broken gas lines, leaking hydraulic

body: To effectively carry out isolation procedures, workers must be able to recognise and use the safety signs and tags used in the workplace. Below are common examples used in electrotechnology workplaces:

_graphic.alt: Description

body:
Tags for electrical and non-electrical isolationTags for electrical and non-electrical isolation
TagDescription
Danger tagsWarn workers that equipment or systems are currently isolated and must not be turned on Commonly used for electrical switchboards, machine control panels, circuit breakers, gas valves and hydraulic systems during maintenance
Out-of-service tagsIndicate that a device, system or equipment should not be powered on because it is damaged, unsafe or undergoing repairs Used for faulty electrical panels, broken gas lines, leaking hydraulic machinery and compromised mechanical systems
Lockout tags (Personal lockout tags)Ensure that only the worker who placed the tag can remove it, preventing accidental activation Commonly used alongside lockout devices on electrical panels, gas valves and pressurised systems to restrict operation until maintenance is completed
Safety signs and barricades for isolationSafety signs and barricades for isolation
Safety signDescription
Restricted Access SignsPrevent unauthorised entry and warn of potential dangers Placed near high-risk work zones, such as areas with live electrical work, high-voltage systems, pressurised pipes or hazardous chemicals
Physical BarriersUsed to block access to hazardous areas where isolation is in effect Includes temporary fencing, cones and warning tapes

_graphic.alt: Placed near high-risk work zones, such as areas with live electrical w

body: • In some workplaces, danger tags are used alongside barricades or restricted access signs to strengthen isolation procedures. This ensures better visibility and prevents unauthorised access, reducing the risk of accidental re-energisation or exposure to hazardous conditions.

_graphic.alt: Clear the work area.

body:
Preparing and Confirming Isolation Procedures

Both electrical and non-electrical isolation must be carried out according to general workplace procedures. These procedures may vary depending on the organisation, the type of work and the risks involved.

Isolation procedures must be performed by an authorised person, such as a licensed electrician, technician or someone who has been formally trained and recognised as competent by the workplace. They are trained to:

_graphic.alt: Once preparation is complete, the authorised person carries out the el

body:
• Switch off power at isolation points
  • Apply Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) devices
  • Confirm that hazardous energy has been fully removed

_graphic.alt: Prepare tools and PPE while maintaining a safe distance

body:
However, all other workers still play a key role in supporting isolation procedures. They are responsible for:

  • Preparing the work area and themselves before isolation takes place
  • Following instructions from the authorised person or supervisor
  • Confirming that isolation has been completed before beginning any task

_graphic.alt: This confirmation can happen in person, by radio or during a pre-start

body:
Before isolation begins, workers must make sure they and their work area are ready. This preparation helps ensure the isolation process can be carried out safely and that there are no extra hazards present when work starts. Workers can prepare by following these general procedures:

  • Clear the work area.
  • Make sure the workspace is clean and free of unnecessary tools, materials or debris.
  • Remove any tripping hazards or items that may block access to isolation points.
  • Inform others of the upcoming isolation.
  • Let nearby workers or supervisors know that isolation is about to take place.
  • This helps avoid accidental use of the equipment while it is being isolated.
  • Wear the correct PPE.
  • Put on any required personal protective equipment (PPE) such as safety glasses, arc-rated gloves or hearing protection.
  • This ensures the worker is ready to enter the area safely after isolation is confirmed.
  • Check for permits and instructions.
  • Confirm that permits or safety briefings have been completed, if required.
  • This helps the worker understand the task, the hazards involved and their responsibilities.

Once preparation is complete, the authorised person carries out the electrical or non-electrical isolation.

  • The authorised person shuts down the energy source.
  • Electrical: They turn off the power supply at circuit breakers or isolators.
  • Non-electrical: They shut off valves, disconnect mechanical linkages or close pressure sources.
  • The authorised person applies Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) devices.
  • They place locks on switches, valves or control systems to prevent reactivation.
  • Tags are attached to indicate who performed the isolation and when.
  • The authorised person discharges any stored energy.
  • Electrical: They release residual energy from capacitors or static sources.
  • Non-electrical: They vent or drain pressurised systems like gas lines or hydraulics.
  • The authorised person tests for complete isolation.
  • Electrical: They use a voltage tester to ensure there is no live power remaining.
  • Non-electrical: They check that pressure gauges show zero or that moving parts have completely stopped.
  • The authorised person secures the area.
  • They place barricades or warning signs around the hazardous zone to stop people from entering.

While the authorised person completes the isolation, the worker may be required to:

  • Stay nearby and wait for confirmation before proceeding
  • Observe the process for learning or verification purposes
  • Prepare tools and PPE while maintaining a safe distance

title: Here is an informative video about electrical isolation: Electrical Isolation in the Workplace
displayTitle: Here is an informative video about electrical isolation: Electrical Isolation in the Workplace

body:
After the isolation, workers must confirm that isolation has been completed by the authorised person before starting any task that involves electrical or non-electrical hazards. This ensures that all hazardous energy sources are fully shut down and secured before workers begin repairs, maintenance or installation tasks.

To confirm isolation:

  • Check that Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) devices are in place.
  • Look for locks and tags on isolators, switches or valves.
  • Make sure they are secure and labelled with the name of the authorised person.
  • Ask the authorised person for confirmation.
  • Speak directly with the person who performed the isolation
  • You can ask questions like:
  • ‘Has the system been tested with a voltage tester?’
  • ‘Has all the pressure been released from the pipes?’
  • ‘Are the moving parts fully secured?’
  • This confirmation can happen in person, by radio or during a pre-start briefing
  • Follow workplace procedures.
  • Some workplaces may require:
  • A formal sign-off from a supervisor
  • Submission of an isolation verification form or digital checklist
  • Always follow your site's instructions before starting work.

Once isolation has been verified and confirmed, workers must not remove, bypass or interfere with isolation controls until work is fully completed. If additional tasks arise or changes are needed, workers must:

  • Reconfirm isolation with an authorised person before making any adjustments.
  • Follow re-energisation procedures only after getting formal approval from the supervisor or WHS officer.

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 7 min

You will learn how to inspect and use tools, equipment and chemicals correctly to prevent accidents and injuries.

title: Check Tools, Equipment and Chemicals for Safety
body: Before starting any electrical work, workers must ensure that all tools, equipment and chemicals are safe to use and function correctly. Faulty tools or improperly handled chemicals can cause electrical hazards, fires or serious injuries. Following workplace procedures and safety regulations helps prevent accidents and ensures compliance with WHS laws.
displayTitle: Check Tools, Equipment and Chemicals for Safety

_graphic.alt: Manual tools

title: 1.5.1 Tools and Equipment
body: In electrotechnology, tools are handheld or portable devices used by electricians to install, repair or test electrical systems. Below are examples of tools commonly used for electrical work:

_graphic.alt: Used for tightening and loosening screws on electrical terminals and c

body:
ToolsPurpose
Manual toolsManual tools
Insulated screwdriverUsed for tightening and loosening screws on electrical terminals and components while providing protection from electric shocks
PliersUsed for gripping, twisting, bending and cutting electrical wires Common types include long-nose pliers for precision work and side-cutting pliers for cutting wires

_graphic.alt: Manual tools

body:
ToolsPurpose
Manual toolsManual tools
Wire stripperDesigned to remove insulation from electrical wires without damaging the conductors
Crimping toolUsed to attach connectors to electrical wires by compressing them securely Essential for making reliable electrical connections
Power toolsPower tools
Electric drillUsed for drilling holes in walls, panels or junction boxes to install electrical conduits, cables or fixtures
GrinderUsed to cut, polish or smooth metal surfaces, such as conduit pipes or grounding rods
Reciprocating sawUsed for cutting through electrical conduit, drywall or other materials when installing wiring systems

_graphic.alt: Designed to remove insulation from electrical wires without damaging t

body:
ToolsPurpose
Testing toolsTesting tools
MultimeterMeasure voltage, current and resistance in electrical circuits Essential for diagnosing faults and ensuring circuits function properly
Voltage testerQuickly check whether a wire or circuit is live before working on it, preventing accidental electric shock
Clamp meterMeasure electrical current in a conductor without direct contact by clamping around the wire, making it safer and easier to check circuit loads

_graphic.alt: Power tools

body: In electrical work, equipment refers to larger devices used to install, maintain, test or repair electrical systems. Unlike hand tools, equipment is often powered, more complex and requires specialised training to operate. Below are examples of equipment:

_graphic.alt: Used for drilling holes in walls, panels or junction boxes to install

body:
EquipmentPurpose
Installation equipmentInstallation equipment
Conduit benderUsed to shape electrical conduits for specific installations
Cable pulling machinesUsed to install cables through conduits or walls
Cable crimping machineUsed for securing electrical connections

_graphic.alt: Used to cut, polish or smooth metal surfaces, such as conduit pipes or

body:
EquipmentPurpose
Maintenance and repair equipmentMaintenance and repair equipment
Insulation resistance testerLarger devices used to measure insulation quality in electrical systems
Earth fault loop testerUsed to measure earth resistance and confirm electrical safety
Portable RCD testerUsed to test the functionality of residual current devices (RCDs)
Testing equipmentTesting equipment
Circuit tracerUsed to locate electrical cables and identify faults
Ground fault detectorUsed to identify grounding issues in electrical circuits
Portable power quality analyserUsed to measure voltage, frequency and harmonic distortions in electrical systems

_graphic.alt: Essential for diagnosing faults and ensuring circuits function properl

body:
Before using any tools or equipment in electrical work, you must inspect them to ensure they are safe and functioning correctly. Proper checks help prevent accidents, equipment damage and non-compliance with safety regulations. You must follow your workplace procedures and regulatory requirements, such as AS/NZS 3760 (Testing of electrical equipment), to check and maintain tools and equipment properly. Below are general procedures for checking tools and equipment:

Conduct a visual inspection. Carefully examine the tool or equipment for any visible signs of damage, such as:

Worn-out grips or handles

_graphic.alt: Quickly check whether a wire or circuit is live before working on it,

body:
Cracked or broken parts

Exposed or frayed wiring

Loose or missing screws and bolts

_graphic.alt: Check controls. Test switches and buttons for proper response.

body:
Signs of overheating, corrosion or rust

Perform electrical safety checks. For tools and equipment that require electricity, you must:

Test and tag according to AS/NZS 3760 using a portable appliance tester (PAT).

_graphic.alt: Some workplaces may require a formal sign-off from an authorised perso

body:
Check grounding – Ensure the earth pin or grounding wire is intact.

Confirm residual current device (RCD) protection – Press the RCD test button to confirm it cuts power.

Conduct functional testing. Test the tool or equipment to ensure it works as expected. You must:

Check controls. Test switches and buttons for proper response.

Test under load. Operate the tool under typical conditions where safe.

Watch for issues. Look for unusual noises, overheating or vibration.

Check for proper calibration. Ensure the accuracy of the tool or equipment. You must:

Use a known reference value. Test against a known voltage or load.

Follow manufacturer guidelines. Ensure calibration is up to date.

Check the calibration certificate. Confirm the tool is certified.

Verify safety features and insulation.

Check insulation. Look for cracks or exposed wiring.

Test safety features such as:

Emergency stops

Overload protection

Automatic shut-off

Safety interlocks

Confirm overload protection. Test under load to see if it trips correctly.

Follow workplace reporting and replacement procedures. If a tool or piece of equipment is damaged or fails safety checks:

Report it to a supervisor or WHS officer immediately.

Tag the item as ‘out of service’ and follow workplace guidelines for repair or disposal.

Never attempt to repair electrical equipment unless authorised and trained to do so.

Here are some additional guidelines:

Tools and equipment that fail inspection should never be used until repaired or replaced.

Follow your workplace’s tagging system to ensure faulty items are not accidentally used.

Some workplaces may require a formal sign-off from an authorised person before returning tools or equipment to service.

title: ✅ Activity 1.4 — electrical tool check
body: You’ll do this for real with your trainer. Practise the steps here: tick everything that a safe electrical tool check includes.

title: Activity 1.4 — Electrical tool check
body: Tick everything that a safe electrical tool check includes.

_items.text: Visually inspect the electrical tool for signs of damage or wear (e.g. checking the plug, looking for exposed wires, checking that there are no missing parts).
Test the tool to confirm it is in good working condition (e.g. switching it on, pressing buttons or triggers, checking safety features and movement).
Verbally confirm whether the tool is safe to use, based on inspection and testing (e.g. confirming it is functioning properly, reporting any issues).
Repair any damage yourself on the spot, even if you are not authorised.
Use the tool anyway if you are in a hurry.

_feedback.correct: Exactly right — Look, Test, Confirm. Inspect it, test that it works, then confirm out loud whether it is safe.

_incorrect.final: Remember the routine: Look → Test → Confirm. Never repair a tool yourself unless authorised, and never use a tool that fails the check.

_partlyCorrect.final: You’re close. A safe check is Look → Test → Confirm. Don’t repair tools yourself unless authorised, and never use one that fails.

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 15 min

Classify, label, store and handle chemicals safely (incl. GHS pictograms and SDS).

title: 1.5.2 Chemicals
body:
Chemicals are essential for completing electrical work. However, they can pose health and safety risks if not handled properly. Understanding the classification, labelling, storage requirements and how to interpret Safety Data Sheets (SDS) is crucial for maintaining a safe work environment.

In Australia, the handling and use of chemicals in the workplace are regulated under:

WHS Act – Sets out general legal requirements for handling hazardous chemicals, including proper storage and use of solvents and cleaning agents in electrical work.

_graphic.alt: Propane (used in soldering)

body:
WHS Regulations – Provides detailed rules for labelling, storing and handling chemicals. Electricians must follow these when working with solvents, insulation resins and other hazardous substances.

Code of Practice: Managing risks of hazardous chemicals in the workplace – Offers practical guidance on safely handling and disposing of chemicals like cleaning sprays, lead-based solder and insulation resins.

Electricians commonly work with the following chemicals in the field:

_graphic.alt: Contact cleaner, insulating varnish

body:
ChemicalDescription
Soldering fluxUsed for electrical soldering; may cause respiratory and skin irritation
Contact cleanersUsed to clean electrical components; often flammable and may emit harmful fumes
Insulating varnishUsed for coating motor windings and electrical components; may be flammable and emit fumes
Coolants and lubricantsUsed for cutting, drilling or cooling electrical components; may be toxic if inhaled
Adhesives and sealantsUsed for bonding electrical components; some may contain volatile organic compounds (VOCs)
Battery acidFound in backup power systems and emergency lighting; corrosive and can cause burns

_graphic.alt: Oxygen used in certain equipment

body:
Hazardous Substances and Dangerous Goods

In general, hazardous substances and dangerous goods are materials that can pose risks to health, safety or the environment. These materials include, but are not limited to, chemicals, fuels and gases. In the context of electrical work, many hazardous substances and dangerous goods take the form of chemicals used in installation, maintenance and repair tasks. Below are their definitions:

  • Hazardous substances – These are materials that can cause harm to health, such as skin irritation, respiratory issues, burns or long-term illnesses (e.g. cancer). Exposure can occur through inhalation, ingestion or skin contact.
  • Example: Soldering flux – Causes respiratory irritation when fumes are inhaled.
  • Dangerous goods – These are materials that pose immediate physical hazards such as flammability, explosiveness or corrosiveness.
  • Example: Contact cleaner – Highly flammable and can emit harmful fumes.

_graphic.alt: Compressed air in pneumatic tools

body:
The Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labelling of Chemicals (GHS) is an international system used to classify and label hazardous substances and dangerous goods. It ensures that workers can quickly identify hazards and understand how to handle chemicals safely.

In Australia, the GHS is adopted under the:

  • Work Health and Safety (WHS) Act 2011
  • Work Health and Safety (WHS) Regulations 2011
  • Code of Practice: Managing Risks of Hazardous Chemicals in the Workplace

_graphic.alt: Battery acid, cleaning agents

body:
Under GHS, substances are grouped into three main hazard types:

Each hazard classification comes with:

  • Pictograms – These are symbols that help workers instantly recognise the type of hazard.
  • A signal word – This shows how serious the hazard is. There are only two:
  • ‘Danger’ – used for more severe hazards
  • ‘Warning’ – used for less severe hazards
  • Only one signal word is used per label, even if the chemical has many hazards. If both apply, Danger is used.
  • Hazard statements – These explain what the hazard is and how serious it is. A single chemical can have many hazard statements if it has more than one risk.

_graphic.alt: Pesticides (if used in electrical infrastructure)

body: Below are the nine pictograms that represent the physical, health and environmental hazards of chemicals under GHS:

_graphic.alt: Soldering flux, contact cleaner

body:
PictogramSignal wordExample hazard statementExample in electrical work
GHS01—Exploding bomb Explosion, fire, blast or projection hazardDangerH200: Unstable explosivePropane (used in soldering)
GHS02—Flame Flammable liquids, vapour, solids and gases; including self-heating and self-igniting substancesDangerH225: Highly flammable liquid and vapourContact cleaner, insulating varnish
GHS03—Flame over circle Oxidising liquids, solids and gases, may cause or intensify fireDangerH272: May intensify fire; oxidiserOxygen used in certain equipment
GHS04—Gas cylinder Gases under pressureWarningH280: Contains gas under pressure; may explode if heatedCompressed air in pneumatic tools
GHS05—Corrosion Corrosive chemicals, may cause severe skin and eye damage and may be corrosive to metalsDangerH314: Causes severe skin burns and eye damageBattery acid, cleaning agents
GHS06—Skull and crossbones Fatal or toxic if swallowed, inhaled or in contact with skin.DangerH300: Fatal if swallowedPesticides (if used in electrical infrastructure)
GHS07—Exclamation mark Low level toxicity. This includes respiratory, skin and eye irritation, skin sensitisers and chemicals harmful if swallowed, inhaled or in contact with skin.WarningH315: Causes skin irritationSoldering flux, contact cleaner
GHS08—Health Hazard Chronic health hazards; this includes aspiratory and respiratory hazards, carcinogenicity, mutagenicity and reproductive toxicity.DangerH350: May cause cancerIsocyanates (in certain sealants)
GHS09—Environment Hazardous to aquatic life and the environment.NoneH410: Very toxic to aquatic life with long-lasting effectsCoolants and lubricants

_graphic.alt: Isocyanates (in certain sealants)

body:
Based on Using the GHS, used under CC BY 4.0. Commonwealth of Australia

Chemicals must be labelled according to the GHS labelling system. Labels provide important information to help workers handle chemicals safely. A GHS-compliant label includes:

Pictograms

_graphic.alt: Supplier details – Name, address and contact information of the manufa

body:
Signal words

Hazard statements

Product identifier – Name of the chemical

_graphic.alt: Even though the ADG Code focuses on the safe transport of dangerous go

body:
Precautionary statements – Instructions for safe handling, storage and disposal

Supplier details – Name, address and contact information of the manufacturer or supplier

_graphic.alt: Explosive

title: Further reading
body: The link below contains examples of GHS-compliant labels: GHS labelling & ANU GHS label audit https://rses.anu.edu.au/~jcali/ghs/index.html

While GHS is used to classify and label chemicals in the workplace, the Australian Dangerous Goods (ADG) Code applies to chemicals during transport, such as when they are delivered to a site or moved between locations.

Even though the ADG Code focuses on the safe transport of dangerous goods, it is still relevant to electrical technicians because:

Chemicals like gas cylinders and solvents are often delivered to or moved around worksites, substations and workshops

_graphic.alt: Explosive

body:
ADG labels may appear on drums, cylinders or delivery vehicles during routine tasks

Recognising these labels helps technicians receive, unpack and temporarily store chemicals safely before use

ADG labels (also called class labels or placards) are used to identify dangerous goods during transport. These labels:

_graphic.alt: Organic Peroxide

body:
Are diamond-shaped and colour-coded (e.g. red for flammable, yellow for oxidising)

Include a class number (1 to 9) showing the type of danger (e.g. 3 = flammable liquid)

Use pictograms like flames, gas cylinders or skulls to show the main hazard

_graphic.alt: Organic Peroxide

body:
Although ADG and GHS have different purposes, they are closely linked. A single chemical may carry both ADG and GHS labels—one for transport and one for workplace use.

The table below shows how GHS classes align with equivalent ADG classifications:

_graphic.alt: Organic Peroxide

GHS05—CorrosionCorrosive to metals
