_graphic.alt: Organic Peroxide

body:
Based on Labelling hazardous chemicals, used under CC BY 4.0. Commonwealth of Australia

Considering the scope of your role, you are not expected to classify chemicals or handle transport documents. But you must be able to:

Recognise GHS pictograms and warning labels on chemical containers

_graphic.alt: Organic Peroxide

body:
Understand what ADG signs or placards mean when receiving or storing deliveries

Know what to do if you see damaged labels or leaking containers

Use the right PPE and follow workplace procedures for handling chemicals

_graphic.alt: Organic Peroxide

body:
Safe storage of chemicals is regulated under the:

WHS Regulations – Outlines general requirements for chemical storage.

Code of Practice: Managing Risks of Hazardous Chemicals in the Workplace – Provides practical guidance on chemical storage.

_graphic.alt: Oxidising gas

body:
Following these regulations is essential because improper storage of chemicals can lead to serious safety risks, including fires, explosions, chemical spills and long-term health effects. It can also result in damage to the equipment, workplace and the environment. That is why workers must follow correct chemical storage procedures.

Good chemical storage practices include:

Using the correct container for each chemical – Chemicals must be kept in containers made to hold them safely. These are usually made from chemical-resistant plastic or metal. Never use containers that are damaged or do not have labels.

_graphic.alt: Toxic gas

body:
Placing chemicals in appropriate storage areas – Store chemicals in the correct place, such as fire-rated cabinets for flammable liquids or ventilated rooms for strong fumes.

Labelling all chemicals clearly and using warning signs – Each container should have a clear label showing what is inside and any hazards. Storage areas must also have warning signs to alert others (e.g. ‘FLAMMABLE’ or ‘CORROSIVE’).

Electricians may only use small amounts of chemicals. Still, these chemicals must be stored properly. Here are some common safety practices for storing chemicals in electrical work:

_graphic.alt: Corrosive to metals

body:
• Store flammable liquids in a flammable liquids cabinet (fire-rated).
  • Keep oxidising agents away from flammable chemicals.
  • Store corrosive substances (like battery acid) in corrosion-resistant containers.
  • Ensure gas cylinders are stored upright and secured to prevent tipping.
  • Keep incompatible chemicals separated to avoid dangerous reactions.
  • Maintain clear labels on all containers and ensure emergency spill kits are nearby.

_graphic.alt: Acute toxic gas

body:
In workplaces that store larger amounts of hazardous chemicals, extra safety measures must also be followed. These measures are the responsibility of the person conducting a business or undertaking (PCBU) or the workplace supervisor.

One of these measures is keeping a hazardous chemical manifest—a written list that includes:

  • The name of each chemical
  • How much is stored
  • Where it is stored
  • Emergency contact details

_graphic.alt: Acute toxic gas

body:
This manifest helps emergency services respond quickly in case of a fire, leak or explosion. It also supports safe storage by tracking which chemicals are present and where they are located.

Another requirement is the use of placards—large warning signs placed at building entrances or chemical storage areas if the amount of chemicals exceeds certain legal limits. These signs alert emergency responders to the type of hazards inside, such as flammable gas or toxic liquid.

These requirements only apply if the total amount of hazardous chemicals on site goes beyond the thresholds listed in Schedule 11 of the WHS Regulations. When this happens, the PCBU must also give written notice to the local WHS regulator.

_graphic.alt: Environmental hazard

body:
Although you are not responsible for creating manifests or placing placards, you must still:

Safety Data Sheets

A Safety Data Sheet (SDS), previously called a Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS), is a document that provides detailed information about a chemical, including its hazards, safe handling procedures and emergency response measures. SDS documents are essential for managing chemical safety in the workplace and ensuring compliance with Australian regulations.

_graphic.alt: Miscellaneous dangerous goods

body:
In Australia, the requirement to provide and use an SDS is outlined under:

  • Model Work Health and Safety (WHS) Regulations 2011 – Requires that hazardous chemicals used in the workplace must have an up-to-date SDS prepared in accordance with the GHS.
  • Code of Practice: Managing Risks of Hazardous Chemicals in the Workplace – Provides guidance on how to use, store and reference SDS information.

_graphic.alt: Infectious

body:
Employers must ensure that SDS documents are available and accessible to all workers who handle chemicals. Workers are responsible for reading and following the safety information outlined in the SDS before using any chemical.

An SDS typically includes the following key sections:

  • Identification – Chemical name, product code, manufacturer details and emergency contact information.
  • Hazard identification – Description of health risks (e.g. toxic, corrosive) and physical risks (e.g. flammable, explosive) using GHS hazard classifications.
  • Composition – List of chemical ingredients and their concentration levels.
  • First aid measures – Instructions on how to treat exposure (e.g. flushing eyes with water, respiratory assistance).
  • Handling and storage – Guidelines for safe use, ventilation requirements and compatible storage recommendations.
  • Exposure controls and PPE – Recommended protective equipment (e.g. gloves, eye protection, respirators).
  • Spill and disposal procedures – Steps for cleaning up spills and disposing of chemicals safely in accordance with environmental regulations.

_graphic.alt: Radioactive

title: Further reading
body: For more information on safety data sheets, click the link below: Safety data sheets https://www.safeworkaustralia.gov.au/safety-topic/hazards/chemicals/safety-data-sheets

How to Check Chemicals for Safety Before Use

Before using any chemicals in the workplace, workers must perform safety checks to prevent exposure, accidents and environmental damage. Proper handling ensures compliance with workplace procedures and regulatory requirements. Below are general guidelines:

  • Identify and confirm the chemical. Confirm that the chemical that will be used for the task is correct by checking:

_graphic.alt: Electricians may only use small amounts of chemicals. Still, these che

body:
The product name and manufacturer details

The purpose of the chemical and its compatibility with the task

Expiry date (if applicable)

_graphic.alt: Spill and disposal procedures – Steps for cleaning up spills and dispo

body:
If unsure about the chemical's suitability, consult the supervisor or WHS officer.

  • Review the Safety Data Sheet (SDS). Check the SDS to understand the chemical's hazards, safe handling requirements, recommended PPE and emergency measures. Ensure that the SDS is current (within 5 years) and accessible.
  • Inspect the container. Check the container for:

_graphic.alt: If emergency equipment is missing or not working, report it before sta

body:
Cracks, leaks or corrosion

Faded, missing or unclear labels

Loose or damaged caps or lids

Ensure that the container is properly sealed and not compromised.

  • Confirm proper labelling. Ensure that the container label matches the information in the SDS. Look for GHS hazard symbols, precautionary statements and handling instructions.
  • Ensure correct PPE is available. Confirm that the correct PPE is available and in good condition:

Safety goggles for eye protection

Chemical-resistant gloves for hand protection

Respirator for vapours or fumes

Protective clothing (e.g. coveralls)

If the required PPE is not available or is damaged, do not use the chemical—report it immediately.

  • Verify emergency and first aid measures. Ensure that first aid kits, eye wash stations and spill kits are accessible and in working condition.

Confirm that emergency procedures are understood:

How to respond to chemical exposure

How to report incidents

How to evacuate in case of a chemical spill or fire

If emergency equipment is missing or not working, report it before starting work.

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 7 min

This section outlines the types of PPE used in electrical work and how to select and use them properly.

title: Use Appropriate Personal Protective Equipment
body: • Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) is used to keep people safe while they work. It helps to protect workers from injuries and accidents that can happen on the job. PPE prevents serious injuries or even saves lives. For instance, wearing insulated gloves protects against electric shock when working with live electrical parts.
Here are some examples of PPE and their purposes:

PPEPurpose
Insulated glovesProtects hands from electric shock when handling live electrical components or equipment
Arc-rated clothingProvides protection against burns from electrical arcs. Clothing should have the correct arc rating for the level of potential arc flash exposure
Safety boots (electrical hazard rated)Insulated to prevent electric shock and protect against falling objects
Safety glasses or face shieldsProtects the eyes and face from debris, sparks and electrical arcs
Hearing protectionUsed when working near loud equipment or where high noise levels are present
RespiratorsUsed when working in areas with dust, fumes, vapours or insufficient oxygen levels to protect against inhalation of harmful airborne substances

_graphic.alt: Before using PPE, you must inspect it carefully to ensure it is in goo

body:
Here are some examples of different tasks and the appropriate PPE for each:

  • Handling heavy loads – When lifting and moving heavy electrical components such as cables or panels, electricians should wear insulated gloves to provide a secure grip and protect against cuts or blisters. A back support belt can help reduce the risk of back strain.
  • Working with hazardous materials – If handling chemicals such as solvents, cleaning agents or insulating varnish, electricians should wear chemical-resistant gloves and safety goggles to protect their skin and eyes. A respirator may be necessary to prevent inhalation of toxic fumes.
  • Working at heights – When working on ladders or elevated platforms to install lighting or overhead wiring, electricians should wear a safety harness and lanyard. Non-slip boots should also be used to maintain stability and prevent falls.

Wearing the right PPE is essential for protecting yourself from electrical hazards such as electric shock, burns, arc flashes and exposure to hazardous substances. Choosing appropriate PPE involves considering the type of work, the risks involved and workplace safety requirements.

  • When selecting PPE for electrical work, you need to consider the following:
  • Hazards encountered – Identify potential hazards such as live wires, arc flash, high-voltage equipment and chemicals to determine the type of protection needed.
  • Fit and comfort – PPE must fit properly to provide effective protection. Ill-fitting gloves, face shields or clothing can expose workers to electrical and physical hazards.
  • Training – Workers must be trained to understand how to choose and wear PPE properly. Training should cover how to inspect PPE and when it needs to be replaced.
  • Standards compliance – PPE should meet Australian safety standards (e.g. AS/NZS 4836 for electrical work) to ensure it provides adequate protection.

Before using PPE, you must inspect it carefully to ensure it is in good condition and will provide adequate protection. Follow these guidelines based on workplace procedures and safety regulations:

Perform a visual inspection.

Check for cracks, tears, cuts and signs of wear.

_graphic.alt: This order helps prevent contamination and injury. PPE that is more co

body:
For gloves, ensure there are no holes or exposed linings.

Ensure face shields and goggles are free from scratches that could affect visibility.

Test functionality.

Test insulated gloves and tools according to AS/NZS 3760 (Testing of electrical equipment).

Confirm that arc-rated clothing, safety boots and gloves are rated for the voltage level being worked on.

Ensure that RCDs (residual current devices) are in place and working correctly.

Check for cleanliness.

Inspect your PPE for dirt, stains or contamination.

Ensure masks and gloves are clean to prevent the spread of germs, especially when working with electrical components in client areas.

_graphic.alt: Understanding the classification, labelling, storage requirements and

body:
Verify expiry dates.

Check the expiry dates on PPE, such as masks and gloves, before use.

Ensure all PPE is within its valid period, particularly when working with high-voltage equipment or in hazardous conditions.

Follow workplace guidelines.

Report damaged PPE to the supervisor immediately.

Tag and remove damaged PPE from use according to workplace policies.

Use only PPE that meets Australian Standards and workplace guidelines.

The following are steps for properly using your PPE:

Follow manufacturer’s instructions. Always read and follow the instructions provided with your PPE. These guidelines ensure that you are wearing the equipment correctly and that it provides maximum protection. For example, arc-rated clothing must be worn according to the manufacturer’s guidelines to provide proper protection from electrical arcs.

_graphic.alt: Speak with your trainer for additional instructions and guidance.

body:
Put on PPE in the correct order.

Safety boots (electrical hazard-rated) – Start with electrical hazard-rated boots to insulate from the ground and prevent electric shock. Ensure a secure fit for stable footing.

Arc-rated clothing – Wear arc-rated clothing to protect against burns from electrical arcs. Ensure full body coverage and a proper fit.

Insulated gloves – Put on insulated gloves to protect from electric shock. Check for damage and ensure they are rated for the working voltage.

Safety glasses or face shields – Wear safety glasses or a face shield to protect against sparks, debris and arcs. Ensure they fit securely and provide clear vision.

Respirators – If needed, put on your respirator last to avoid inhaling dust or fumes. Ensure a proper seal and that it is rated for the specific hazard.

Check the fit. PPE that is too loose or too tight will not protect you properly. For example, insulated gloves should fit snugly to maintain dexterity while preventing exposure to live parts, but they should not restrict hand movement.

Use PPE mindfully during tasks.

Stay aware. Regularly check your PPE for damage while working. Replace it immediately if you notice tears, cracks or contamination.

_graphic.alt: Speak with your trainer for additional instructions and guidance.

body:
Minimise adjustments. Avoid frequently touching or adjusting your PPE while working on live electrical equipment, as this can compromise its protection.

Use task-appropriate PPE. Wear PPE specific to the electrical task. For example, wear flame-resistant clothing when working with high-voltage systems or heavy-duty gloves when handling rough or sharp materials.

Remove PPE in the correct order. When finished, remove PPE carefully to avoid contamination. Follow this order:

This order helps prevent contamination and injury. PPE that is more contaminated or close to the face (like a respirator or safety glasses) should be removed first, to avoid spreading particles or chemicals. Gloves are removed before touching clothes and arc-rated clothing is taken off before boots, to avoid tripping and ensure safe handling of gear.

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 7 min

The Learner Guide’s key points, plus a chance to re-run the Chapter 1 checks.

title: Key points
body: In this chapter, we learnt: There are different types of work area access permits that may be required based on the specific risks and tasks performed (e.g. confined space, high voltage work). Work health and safety (WHS) laws, regulations and codes of practice exist to reduce workplace risks, prevent injuries and ensure workers’ safety. Safe work methods are structured procedures and best practices that outline the correct way to perform tasks in a safe, efficient and compliant manner. Isolation means making sure that electrical or mechanical systems are separated from their power sources. Understanding the classification, labelling, storage requirements and how to interpret Safety Data Sheets (SDS) is crucial for using chemicals.

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 6 min

What asbestos is, why it is dangerous, and how to control the risk.

title: Implement Risk Control Work Measures
body:
In electrotechnology workplaces, workers encounter various hazards that can lead to serious injuries or equipment damage if not properly managed. To effectively apply WHS regulations, it is essential to understand the difference between hazards and risks in this context.

Hazard - Anything that has the potential to cause harm.

Risk - The likelihood that harm will occur due to exposure to a hazard, combined with the severity of the possible injury or damage
Below are examples of hazards and associated risks:

HazardsAssociated risks
Exposed live electrical wiresRisk of electric shock, burns or fatal electrocution if a worker accidentally contacts the wire
Damaged power tools or extension cordsRisk of electrocution or short circuits due to exposed wires or faulty insulation
Poorly maintained ladders or scaffoldsRisk of falls from height, leading to broken bones or serious head injuries

A hazard is always present, but the risk depends on how likely someone is to be exposed to it and whether safety measures are in place.

For example:

A live electrical wire is a hazard, but if it is properly insulated and no one touches it, the risk stays low.

_graphic.alt: This section outlines the importance of keeping the worksite organised

body:
If that same wire is exposed and a worker is nearby without the right safety gear, the risk of electric shock becomes much higher.

Electricians may encounter specific hazards during specialised tasks. The following sections focus on these hazards and how to manage them.

title: 2.1.1 Asbestos
body:
Asbestos is a group of naturally occurring minerals made up of thin, fibrous crystals. It was widely used in construction and electrical installations because of its strength, fire resistance and insulating properties. However, inhaling asbestos fibres can cause serious and life-threatening diseases. Although asbestos has been banned in Australia since December 31, 2003, many older buildings still contain asbestos-containing materials (ACMs).

Asbestos fibres are inhaled when materials containing asbestos are disturbed and release tiny fibres into the air. This can happen during:

Drilling into walls or ceilings that contain asbestos

Cutting or sanding asbestos-containing materials (ACMs)

Removing old insulation around pipes or ducts

Breaking or damaging asbestos cement sheets or tiles

When asbestos fibres are inhaled, they can become trapped in the lungs. Over time, these fibres cause inflammation and scarring, leading to serious health problems such as:

Asbestosis – Scarring of lung tissue, causing breathing difficulties.

Lung cancer – Long-term exposure to asbestos increases the risk of developing lung cancer.

_graphic.alt: Asbestos is a group of naturally occurring minerals made up of thin, f

body:
Mesothelioma – A rare but aggressive cancer affecting the lining of the lungs or abdomen, directly linked to asbestos exposure.

Once asbestos fibres are inhaled, they remain in the lungs permanently and symptoms may take decades to appear.

The following are common types of asbestos-containing materials (ACMs) that electricians may encounter:

MaterialLocationElectrical work example
Asbestos cement sheetingRoofs, walls, ceilings, external claddingDrilling into walls or ceilings to install conduit or junction boxes
Asbestos insulation board (AIB)Electrical switchboards, partition walls, ceilingsRemoving or modifying switchboards containing AIB
Textured coatingsCeilings and walls in older homes and commercial buildingsDrilling into ceilings or walls to install light fixtures or switches
Pipe laggingInsulation around hot water pipes and electrical conduitsRunning conduit or cables near or through lagged pipes
Floor tiles and adhesivesVinyl floor tiles, tile adhesivesLifting floor tiles to install conduit underneath
Gaskets and sealsFound in electrical equipment, machinery, pipeworkReplacing or modifying seals during electrical maintenance
Sprayed fireproof coatingsApplied to steel beams, ceilings and wallsDrilling into coated beams to install cable trays or mounts

Supervisors have a legal responsibility under WHS laws to protect workers from asbestos exposure. This includes:

Providing asbestos awareness training – Workers should know how to identify and handle asbestos safely.

Assessing the worksite – Supervisors must confirm whether asbestos is present before starting work.

Developing a safe work method statement (SWMS) – Outlines how to manage asbestos risks.

Ensuring proper protective equipment (PPE) – Respirators, gloves and coveralls should be worn when handling or working near asbestos.

Reporting and monitoring – Any suspected asbestos should be reported immediately and work should stop until the area is assessed.

_graphic.alt: To protect workers, asbestos-containing areas should be clearly marked

body:
To protect workers, asbestos-containing areas should be clearly marked with warning signs, including:

  • Asbestos labels – Labels should include specific warnings and instructions to avoid disturbing the material.

‘Danger – Asbestos’ signs are commonly used on switchboards and equipment containing asbestos.

Site signage – Worksites containing asbestos must have warning signs at entry points. Signs must be highly visible and positioned where workers can easily see them. Common signs include:

‘Warning – Asbestos – Do Not Disturb’

‘Asbestos Removal in Progress – Keep Out’

‘Respiratory Protection Required’

Markings on materials – Asbestos-containing panels and insulation boards are often stamped with identification codes.

If the material was installed before 2003 and is unmarked, it should be treated as asbestos until proven otherwise.

To control risks associated with asbestos, workers must:

  • Check for asbestos before starting work, especially in older buildings.
  • Stop work immediately if asbestos is found and report it to a supervisor.
  • Avoid cutting, drilling or disturbing materials suspected to contain asbestos.
  • Wear a P2 respirator and protective clothing when working near asbestos.
  • Follow the asbestos management plan and signage.

_graphic.alt: Follow the asbestos management plan and signage.

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 4 min

Silica dust hazards and how to control them.

title: 2.1.2 Silica
body:
Silica is a naturally occurring mineral found in sand, rock and soil. In its crystalline form, it is known as crystalline silica or silica dust. It is used in many building materials, including concrete, bricks and tiles. When these materials are disturbed, they release fine silica dust into the air. Silica dust is dangerous because it is small enough to be inhaled and settle deep in the lungs, causing long-term health issues.

Silica dust is released when materials containing crystalline silica are disturbed or broken down during common electrical work activities, such as:

Cutting – Cutting into concrete, tiles or bricks using a saw or grinder releases fine silica particles.

Drilling – Drilling into concrete or brick walls to install conduit or mount electrical fixtures generates airborne silica dust.

Grinding and polishing – Smoothing out rough edges on concrete or tile creates fine dust that becomes airborne.

Demolition and removal – Removing old concrete or tiles can release large amounts of silica dust into the air.

Sweeping and cleaning – Dry sweeping or brushing after cutting or drilling can stir up settled silica dust, making it easy to inhale.

When silica dust is inhaled, the fine particles settle in the lungs and cause inflammation and scarring. Over time, this can lead to serious health issues, including:

Silicosis – Scarring of the lung tissue, which makes breathing difficult and can worsen over time.

Lung cancer – Long-term exposure to silica increases the risk of lung cancer.

Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD) – Narrowing of the airways, leading to persistent coughing and shortness of breath.

Kidney disease – Silica exposure increases the risk of developing chronic kidney disease.

Autoimmune diseases – Long-term exposure can increase the risk of autoimmune disorders like rheumatoid arthritis and lupus.

Symptoms may not appear immediately, but long-term exposure increases the chances of developing severe health problems.

To protect workers from the health risks of silica dust, exposure levels are regulated under Australian safety standards. The Workplace Exposure Standard (WES) for inhalable crystalline silica is 0.05 mg/m³ (milligrams per cubic metre) over an 8-hour time-weighted average (TWA). This means it is the average amount of silica dust a worker can be safely exposed to during a normal workday. This is a very small amount — so small that you cannot see it in the air. Even a little bit of silica dust in the air can go over the safe limit, which is why ventilation and protective equipment are essential.
Crystalline silica is present in many common construction and industrial materials that electricians may encounter, especially during installation and demolition work.

MaterialSilica content (%)Where it is found in electrical work
Concrete25%–70%Cutting or drilling into walls or floors for conduit installation or mounting panels
Bricks10%–30%Drilling into brick walls to install power points or run conduit
Ceramic tiles30%–45%Drilling into tiles when installing switches or lighting fixtures
Mortar10%–20%Removing or cutting through mortar to install electrical boxes or conduits
Granite20%–45%Installing power outlets or lighting in high-end kitchens or bathrooms
Engineered stoneUp to 90%Cutting stone benchtops to install power points or appliances (specialised work)

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 3 min

Recognising and controlling hazardous gases.

title: 2.1.3 Hazardous Gases
body:
Hazardous gases are gases that can harm you or create dangerous conditions when released into the air. They can be toxic, flammable or reduce oxygen levels, causing suffocation. Electricians may come across these gases in confined spaces, industrial sites or when working with gas-insulated equipment.

When hazardous gases are inhaled, they can enter the bloodstream and affect major organs, including the lungs, brain and heart. The type of harm depends on the gas and the level of exposure.

Here are examples of the harm different types of gases can cause:

Toxic gases – Gases like carbon monoxide can prevent oxygen from reaching the blood, leading to dizziness, confusion and death in severe cases.

Flammable gases – Gases like methane and hydrogen can ignite and cause explosions when exposed to sparks or open flames.

Asphyxiant gases – Gases like nitrogen or argon can replace oxygen in the air, leading to suffocation.

Corrosive gases – Gases like chlorine can burn or damage the respiratory tract, causing breathing difficulties.

Electricians may encounter the following gases during installation, maintenance or repair work:

GasesWhere it is found
Sulphur hexafluoride (SF₆)Used as an insulating gas in switchgear and high-voltage equipment
Carbon monoxide (CO)Produced by fuel-powered generators or poorly ventilated equipment
Methane (CH₄)Found near gas lines or in confined spaces with poor ventilation
Hydrogen (H₂)Released from batteries or industrial equipment
Chlorine (Cl₂)Used in some industrial processes

Supervisors have a legal duty under Work Health and Safety (WHS) regulations to protect workers from exposure to hazardous gases. This includes:

Risk assessment – Supervisors must assess the risk of gas leaks and exposure before starting work.

Monitoring air quality – Regular monitoring of gas levels is required, especially in confined spaces.

Providing proper PPE – Workers should be provided with respirators and protective clothing when working around hazardous gases.

Ventilation and gas detection systems – Work areas should have proper ventilation and gas detectors should be installed where hazardous gases may accumulate.

Emergency procedures – Supervisors must ensure workers know how to respond to gas leaks or exposure, including evacuation procedures and first aid.

To control risks related to hazardous gases, workers must:

Test the air for toxic and flammable gases before starting work.

Wear a respirator suited to the type of gas present.

Ensure proper ventilation when working with gas-insulated equipment.

Use a gas detector to monitor air quality throughout the task.

Evacuate the area immediately if gas levels become unsafe.

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 3 min

The dangers of confined spaces and how to work in them safely.

title: 2.1.4 Confined Spaces
body:
A confined space is an area that:

Is not designed for continuous human occupancy – It is usually small or difficult to access.

Has limited entry and exit points – Getting in and out may be difficult.

May have poor ventilation – This can lead to dangerous air quality or low oxygen levels.

Electricians may need to enter confined spaces to install, repair or inspect electrical equipment. Examples of these spaces include:

Cable pits – Underground enclosures used to house electrical cables

Switchgear rooms – Small, enclosed spaces where high-voltage equipment is installed

Ceiling and roof cavities – Tight spaces with limited airflow where cables and lighting are installed

Manholes and tunnels – Used for accessing underground electrical systems

Basements and crawlspaces – Restricted areas where wiring or panels may need to be installed or repaired

Working in confined spaces increases the risk of several hazards, including:

HazardAssociated risksControl measures
Poor air quality (Lack of oxygen or toxic gases like carbon monoxide or methane)Suffocation from low oxygen levels. Poisoning from inhaling toxic gases.Use a gas detector to monitor oxygen and gas levels before and during work. Use ventilation systems to maintain airflow. Provide respiratory protection (e.g. P2 respirator).
Electric shock (Working near live wires or faulty equipment)Serious injury or death from electric shock. Secondary injuries from falling or losing balance.De-energise equipment before starting work. Use insulated tools and wear PPE (e.g. rubber gloves). Ensure proper tag-out and lock-out procedures are followed.
Fire and Explosion (Presence of flammable gases or vapours)Severe burns or injury from fire. Explosion causing structural collapse or debris.Monitor for flammable gases before work begins. Avoid creating sparks — use non-sparking tools. Keep fire extinguishers nearby.
Heat and poor ventilation (Heat buildup in small spaces)Heat exhaustion or heat stroke. Reduced concentration and increased injury risk.Use fans or ventilation systems to cool the space. Take regular breaks and stay hydrated. Monitor worker condition for signs of heat stress.
Restricted movement (Limited space for positioning or working)Muscle strain and fatigue. Increased risk of injury from awkward postures.Use ergonomic tools designed for confined spaces. Plan the task to minimise unnecessary movement. Rotate workers if the task is physically demanding.
Structural collapse (Loose materials or unstable surfaces)Entrapment or crushing injuries. Difficulty escaping in an emergency.Inspect the space for loose or unstable surfaces before entering. Use a permit-to-work system to approve entry. Ensure a standby person is present outside with rescue equipment.

To control risks associated with confined spaces, workers must:

  • Test the air for oxygen levels and hazardous gases before entering.
  • Ventilate the space using fans or extraction systems.
  • Wear a harness and ensure a standby person is present outside.
  • Follow the permit-to-work system before starting the task.
  • Use non-sparking tools to avoid ignition risks.

_graphic.alt: Use non-sparking tools to avoid ignition risks.

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 3 min

Common physical hazards on electrical worksites.

title: 2.1.5 Physical Hazards
body:
Physical hazards are factors in the work environment that can cause harm to the body through contact, exposure or repeated strain. These hazards can lead to both immediate injuries and long-term health problems if not properly managed.

The table below outlines common physical hazards electricians may encounter, the harm they can cause and how this harm occurs:

Physical hazardHarm it can causeHow this harm occurs
Excessive noiseHearing loss Tinnitus (ringing in the ears)Working near loud machinery, generators or drills for long periods without ear protection
VibrationHand-arm vibration syndrome (HAVS) Numbness or reduced grip strengthUsing power tools like drills and grinders for extended periods
Thermal stress (Exposure to extreme heat or cold)Heat exhaustion or heat stroke Frostbite or hypothermiaWorking in poorly ventilated rooms or outdoor sites during extreme weather
Radiation (Exposure to electromagnetic fields or UV light)Burns Eye damage Long-term health effectsWorking near high-voltage equipment or welding without protective eyewear
LasersEye injuries Skin burnsDirect exposure to laser beams during alignment or cutting tasks
Occupational Overuse Syndrome (OOS) (Repetitive strain injury)Muscle and joint pain Reduced range of motionRepeated use of hand tools and working in awkward positions

Below are control measures for each hazard:

  • Excessive noise

Wear hearing protection (e.g. earmuffs or earplugs) when working near loud equipment.

Limit time spent working with noisy tools like grinders and drills.

Use sound barriers or dampening materials when possible.
• Vibration

Use vibration-reducing gloves when using power tools.

Limit the duration of using vibrating tools — rotate tasks when possible.

Maintain and inspect tools regularly to reduce excessive vibration.
• Thermal stress

Wear heat-resistant or cold-weather protective gear as needed.

Take regular breaks in a shaded or cool area.

Stay hydrated when working in hot environments.

Use ventilation or cooling systems in hot work areas.

  • Radiation

Use protective shielding or barriers when working near high-voltage equipment.

Wear UV-blocking goggles and protective clothing when exposed to UV light.

Follow signage and safety instructions in areas with radiation hazards.
• Lasers

Wear laser safety goggles rated for the specific type of laser in use.

Ensure the laser beam is properly contained and directed away from workers.

Follow manufacturer guidelines and safety procedures when using lasers.
• Occupational Overuse Syndrome (OOS)

Use ergonomically designed tools to reduce strain.

Take regular breaks to stretch and rest muscles.

Rotate tasks to avoid repetitive strain on the same muscle groups.
Maintain proper posture when working in tight spaces.

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 2 min

Workplace factors that can affect mental health and safety.

title: 2.1.6 Psychological Hazards
body:
Psychological hazards are factors in the work environment that can negatively affect a worker's mental and emotional well-being. These hazards include:

  • Stress

High workloads, tight deadlines and pressure to complete tasks quickly can cause stress.

Stress can reduce concentration, increase the risk of mistakes and contribute to long-term mental health issues.

Example: An electrician is pressured to complete a large wiring job within a tight deadline, leading to anxiety and errors in wiring connections.

The following are ways to minimise stress:

Take regular breaks to stay focused and reduce mental strain.

Communicate with your supervisor if the workload is unmanageable.

Ask for help or clarification if unsure about a task.

  • Fatigue

Working long shifts or overnight can reduce alertness and increase the risk of accidents.

Poor sleep patterns or excessive overtime can impair judgment and reaction time.

Example: An electrician working back-to-back shifts mistakenly connects wires incorrectly due to lack of sleep.

The following are ways to avoid fatigue:

Recognise signs of fatigue and stop work if you feel overly tired.

Follow shift rotation schedules and take rest periods when available.

Report fatigue to your supervisor and ask for support if needed.

  • Drugs and alcohol

Substance abuse impairs judgment, reaction time and motor skills.

Alcohol or drug use increases the risk of injury and unsafe behaviour.

Example: An electrician under the influence of alcohol mistakenly touches a live wire, causing an electric shock.

The following are measures to control risks related to drugs and alcohol:

Avoid alcohol and drug use before and during work.

Report any concerns about substance use affecting work safety.

If you feel unfit to work due to substance use, notify your supervisor and step away from the task.

Understanding the different types of hazards electricians face is essential for maintaining a safe work environment. Being aware of these hazards helps you recognise risks early and apply the right control measures to prevent injuries and health issues.

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 12 min

This section explains how to apply the hierarchy of control to eliminate or reduce workplace risks.

title: 2.1.7 Risk Management
body:
Risk management is a structured process used to identify, assess and control risks in the workplace. In electrotechnology workplaces, effective risk management is essential to protect workers from hazards such as electric shock, arc flashes, burns and equipment failure. It ensures that work is performed safely and that employers and workers comply with Work Health and Safety (WHS) regulations and industry standards.

The principles of risk management are outlined in the ISO 31000 – Risk Management Guidelines and reflected in the Model Work Health and Safety (WHS) Act and WHS Regulations. These principles are applied to ensure that the process of identifying and controlling risks is consistent and effective.

Key principles include:

The process of managing risks in electrotechnology workplaces follows a structured framework based on the Code of Practice: Managing electrical risks in the workplace. The steps are:

Identify hazards – Find anything that could cause harm in the workplace (e.g. live wires, faulty equipment). (Specific hazards will be covered in detail in a later chapter.)

Assess risks – Determine how likely it is that harm will happen and how severe the consequences could be.

Control risks – Apply the most effective control measures to eliminate or minimise the risk.

Monitor and review – Regularly check that control measures are working effectively and update them if necessary.

Risk Assessment

Risk assessment is the process of evaluating the likelihood and severity of harm from identified hazards. It helps electricians and supervisors to prioritise risks and decide on the most effective control measures.

You are not required to assess risks. However, it is important to understand what these terms mean so you can follow safety instructions and workplace procedures correctly.

Likelihood - How probable it is that harm will occur due to the hazard.
• Example: A frayed extension cord on a construction site is highly likely to cause electric shock or a short circuit if not repaired.

Severity - The seriousness of the harm that could result, ranging from mild discomfort to severe injury or long-term disability.

  • Example: Accidentally cutting a live wire could result in burns, electric shock or fatal electrocution.

Understanding these terms also helps electricians interpret risk assessment documentation (e.g. risk register, risk assessment form) since they often refer to these for guidance. Supervisors or WHS officers may provide specific guidelines on identifying hazards and risks and applying control measures.

There are five levels of likelihood:

LevelDescriptorDescription
AAlmost certainExpected to occur in most circumstances
BLikelyWill probably occur in most circumstances
COccasionallyMight occur in some circumstances
DUnlikelyCould occur but not expected
ERareMay occur only in exceptional circumstances
There are five levels of consequence – the higher the level, the more severe the consequence:

LevelDescriptorDescription
1InsignificantNegligible impact on safety or workflow
2MinorSmall impact, may cause discomfort but no significant injury
3ModerateNoticeable impact, may result in injury requiring minimal medical attention
4MajorSignificant impact, could lead to serious injury or disruption
5SevereCritical impact, may result in permanent injury or fatality

The table below is used to determine the overall rating of the risk:

Risk Rating Table (or Risk Matrix)

LikelihoodSeveritySeveritySeveritySeveritySeverity
LikelihoodInsignificant 1Minor 2Moderate 3Major 4Severe 5
A (Almost certain)HighHighVery HighVery HighVery High
B (Likely)ModerateHighHighVery HighVery High
C (Moderate)LowModerateHighVery HighVery High
D (Unlikely)LowLowModerateHighVery High
E (Rare)LowLowModerateHighHigh

Each risk rating provides guidance on how to respond to risks:

Low – Risks in this category can generally be accepted. Regular monitoring and basic controls are sufficient to manage them.

Moderate – These risks require attention. Implement appropriate control measures and monitor the situation to ensure it does not escalate.

High – Immediate action is needed to reduce the risk. This may involve implementing additional controls or modifying current procedures to ensure safety.

  • Very high – These risks demand urgent attention. Stop the task or activity until the risk can be effectively controlled. Consult with supervisors or WHS officers to implement strong control measures

Examples:

Low Risk: An electrician installing a power point with insulated gloves and a circuit breaker installed would be considered low risk.

Moderate Risk: Testing an electrical panel while wearing PPE but without confirming the circuit is de-energised increases the level of risk.

High Risk: Working on live wires without appropriate insulation or PPE poses a high risk of electric shock or injury.

Very High Risk: Repairing a high-voltage line without RCD protection or lockout/tagout procedures creates a life-threatening risk.

Risk assessments must be properly documented to comply with WHS requirements. This includes documents such as Safe Work Method Statements (SWMS), Job Safety Analyses (JSA) and permits-to-work. These documents will be discussed in detail in a later subchapter.

Risk Control

Once the risks are identified and assessed, workers must implement control measures to eliminate or reduce the risks. Risk controls are measures taken to reduce or eliminate workplace hazards and risks. Risk controls must follow the hierarchy of control to ensure they are applied systematically and effectively, in line with WHS laws and workplace procedures.

Sourced from Managing risks, used under CC BY 4.0. Commonwealth of Australia

The hierarchy of control includes six key levels of risk control, ranked from the most effective (elimination) to the least effective (PPE). Electricians should apply these controls in order, starting with elimination whenever possible.

Hierarchy of controlExample
Elimination - Removing the hazard entirely.Disconnecting and removing damaged electrical wiring to eliminate the risk of electric shock Decommissioning faulty electrical equipment that cannot be repaired Removing asbestos-containing materials from work areas to eliminate the risk of inhalation
Substitution - Replacing a hazardous process, material or tool with a safer alternative.Using battery-operated tools instead of corded tools to reduce the risk of tripping over cables Replacing a solvent-based contact cleaner with a non-toxic, water-based alternative Installing LED lighting instead of halogen lamps to reduce heat and fire hazards
Isolation - Separating people from the hazard.Installing physical barriers to keep workers away from live electrical components Using lockable enclosures for high-voltage areas Restricting access to switchboards using signage and locked doors
Engineering controls - Modifying the work environment or equipment to reduce risks.Installing insulated barriers or covers over live electrical components to prevent accidental contact Installing arc flash protection devices in switchboards to reduce the severity of an electrical fault Using cable management systems to prevent tripping hazards from exposed wiring

Hierarchy of controlExample
Administrative controls - Establishing policies, training and procedures to guide safe behaviour.Developing a lockout/tagout (LOTO) procedure for isolating electrical equipment before maintenance Providing detailed training on identifying and managing electrical hazards Scheduling regular inspections of electrical equipment to identify and repair faults early
Personal protective equipment (PPE) - Using safety gear to protect workers from residual risks.Wearing insulated gloves and arc-rated clothing when working on live circuits Using safety glasses to protect the eyes from sparks or debris during cutting or drilling Wearing steel-toed boots with non-slip soles to protect against falling objects and slippery surfaces

Example:

If an electrician needs to work on a live electrical panel:

First, attempt to eliminate the risk by shutting off power and using lockout/tagout procedures.

If elimination is not possible, substitute the panel with a safer alternative, if available.

If substitution is not practical, isolate the hazard by restricting access and using physical barriers to separate the live panel from people.

If the hazard cannot be fully isolated, apply engineering controls, such as installing insulated barriers or arc flash protection, to reduce the risk of contact.

Provide clear work instructions and safety briefings (administrative control).

Finally, ensure the electrician is wearing arc-rated gloves, a face shield and other necessary PPE.

To ensure safety and compliance in electrotechnology work, electricians must apply risk control measures at three key stages:

Before starting the task: Identify hazards, ensure the area is safe, check equipment and confirm all team members understand the control measures.

During the task: Follow safe procedures, use protective equipment (e.g. RCDs, LOTO), communicate with the team and manage new risks.

After completing the task: Confirm the area is safe, check for damage and update procedures based on findings.

Following these guidelines ensures that risk control measures are applied effectively and consistently in line with WHS regulations and workplace procedures:

Follow the control measures identified in the risk assessment or permit-to-work.

Inform all team members, supervisors and safety officers about the control measures being implemented.

Ensure that everyone understands their role and the steps involved in managing risks.

Confirm that all required tools and equipment (e.g. multimeters, circuit breakers, PPE) are available and functional.

Follow workplace safety procedures when using tools and handling equipment. (e.g. using proper lifting technique)

Confirm that all control measures are in place and working as intended before starting work.

Be alert to any new hazards that may arise during the task.

Stop work immediately if an unsafe situation develops and report it to the supervisor.

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 7 min

You will learn how to respond quickly and effectively to workplace emergencies, including electrical fires and accidents.

title: Handle Accidents, Fires and Emergencies
body:
Emergency situations are unexpected events that require immediate action to prevent injury, damage or harm. Workers must be prepared to respond quickly and effectively to minimise harm and maintain workplace safety.

To respond effectively to these situations, you must understand both emergency management procedures and first aid. Emergency management procedures help you control the overall situation and prevent further harm, while first aid focuses on providing immediate care to injured or ill individuals. Both will be discussed in the next sections.

title: 2.2.1 Emergency Management Procedures
body:
Emergency management procedures are structured plans and actions designed to respond to unexpected and dangerous situations in the workplace. These procedures are required under several Australian laws and guidelines to ensure that workplaces are prepared to handle emergencies.

Emergency response procedures in electrotechnology workplaces generally follow a common structure, but the specific actions may vary depending on the nature of the situation (accident, emergency or fire). The overall goal is to ensure worker safety, prevent further damage and comply with WHS requirements

The following are general procedures

• Assess the situation. Quickly evaluate the nature and severity of the emergency. Identify any immediate dangers such as exposed wires, live circuits, fire or injured persons. If the area is unsafe (e.g. live electrical equipment or gas leak), keep a safe distance and avoid contact.

_graphic.alt: Ensure personal safety. Protect yourself and others by following evacu

body:
Example: If you see a co-worker collapsed near an exposed wire, assess if the area is live before stepping in to help.

  • Activate the emergency response. Notify the appropriate personnel and raise the alarm if necessary. This may include contacting the site supervisor, safety officer or emergency services (000).

Example: If an arc flash occurs, sound the alarm and notify the supervisor immediately.

  • Ensure personal safety. Protect yourself and others by following evacuation routes or seeking shelter if needed. Ensure that the power supply is isolated if it is safe to do so and use appropriate PPE when responding to hazards.

Example: If a fire starts in an electrical panel, evacuate and use the nearest CO₂ extinguisher if safe to do so.

  • Provide assistance. If it is safe to do so, provide first aid or support to injured persons. Follow DRSABCD guidelines for managing an unconscious casualty (This will be discussed in the next section).

_graphic.alt: Example: After an electric shock incident, fill out an incident report

body:
Example: If a co-worker receives an electric shock and becomes unconscious, ensure the power is off, check for breathing and begin CPR if needed.

  • Follow instructions. Cooperate with emergency personnel and follow workplace emergency procedures. If evacuation is ordered, follow the designated routes and assembly points.
  • Example: If emergency responders arrive, provide them with details about the incident and any actions you’ve taken.
  • Document and report. Complete an incident report and participate in a post-incident debrief if required. Provide accurate details about the cause of the emergency, the actions taken and any recommendations for future prevention.
Example: After an electric shock incident, fill out an incident report detailing the sequence of events and any issues with the safety equipment.

title: Further reading
body: Below are guidelines used by first aiders when responding to emergencies: Guideline 2 – Managing an Emergency https://www.anzcor.org/home/basic-life-support/guideline-2-managing-an-emergency/

These procedures are outlined in an emergency management plan. Under the WHS Act 2011 and WHS Regulations 2011, employers are legally required to have an emergency management plan to protect workers and visitors from harm. Having a clear plan ensures that everyone knows what to do in an emergency, reducing confusion and improving response times.

Below are the typical contents of an emergency management plan:

_graphic.alt: Responsibilities for reviewing and updating the plan

body:
ContentDescription
Emergency proceduresStep-by-step instructions for managing different types of emergencies, such as fires, hazardous gas leaks, medical emergencies
Roles and responsibilitiesDefines the roles and responsibilities of key personnel during an emergency, such as: Site manager – Oversees emergency response and evacuation First aiders – Provide immediate medical care to injured persons Emergency wardens – Direct evacuation and provide guidance to workers
Emergency contact informationA list of internal and external emergency contacts, including: Emergency services (e.g. 000) WHS officer or safety manager Nearest hospital or medical facility
Evacuation proceduresOutlines the steps to evacuate the work area safely in case of an emergency. This includes: Primary and secondary evacuation routes Assembly points Instructions for assisting injured or disabled persons
Communication proceduresSpecifies how to communicate during an emergency to ensure all workers receive timely instructions. This includes: How to raise an alarm (e.g. emergency siren or verbal alert) Methods for updating staff during an emergency (e.g. PA system, mobile phones)
Emergency equipment and locationsLists the location and use of critical emergency equipment. This includes: Location and use of fire extinguishers, first aid kits, emergency exits and spill kits Availability of emergency shutdown procedures for electrical systems
Training and drillsEnsures that workers are prepared to respond to emergencies through regular training and practice drills. This includes: Frequency of emergency drills (e.g. fire, electric shock). Training on emergency procedures and use of emergency equipment Review and feedback sessions after drills or real incidents
Post-incident reporting and reviewEstablishes procedures for documenting and reviewing emergency responses to improve future outcomes. This includes: Incident reporting process How to document lessons learned and update the emergency management plan Responsibilities for reviewing and updating the plan

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 5 min

Fire types, extinguishers and how to respond to a fire.

title: 2.2.2 Responding to Fire
body:
Fire is a common type of emergency in electrical work, often caused by faults such as short circuits or overloaded systems. To respond to electrical fire or other workplace fire, you must:

Raise the alarm. Activate the fire alarm and notify emergency services (000).

Evacuate the area. Follow the fire evacuation plan.

Use fire extinguishers. If safe to do so, use the appropriate fire extinguisher. The correct way to use a fire extinguisher is by following the PASS method:

  • Shut down electrical supply – If possible and safe, isolate the power supply to prevent the fire from spreading.

Close doors and windows – Contain the fire by limiting airflow.

Follow evacuation and reporting procedures – Report the fire and cooperate with emergency responders.

Fires are grouped into different classes based on the fuel or source causing the fire. Understanding these classes helps you choose the correct fire extinguisher and avoid making the fire worse.

Here are the main types of fire classes in Australia:

Class A – Flammable materials: Fires involving everyday materials like paper, wood, cloth and plastic.

Class B – Flammable liquids: Fires started by liquids such as petrol, paint or oil-based products.

Class C – Flammable gases: Fires caused by gases like butane or methane.

Class D – Flammable metals: Fires involving metals such as lithium, potassium or magnesium. These are more common in industrial settings.

Class E – Electrical equipment: Fires that involve live electrical items like computers, power tools or switchboards.

Class F – Cooking fats and oils: Fires from hot oils and fats in fryers and deep pans, typically found in kitchens.

Using the wrong type of extinguisher can make the fire worse or put you at risk. Below are the main types of extinguishers, their uses and how they work. Each type can be identified by its coloured band:

Type of fire extinguisherType of fire extinguisherType of fireMechanism
WaterClass A - Flammable materials.Cools the burning material to remove heat and stop the fire
Dry chemical powder ABEClass A - Flammable materials Class B - Flammable liquids Class E - Electrical equipmentInterrupts the chemical reaction and smothers fire
Dry chemical powder BEClass B - Flammable liquids Class E - Electrical equipmentDisrupts the chemical reaction and smothers the fire without conducting electricity
Carbon dioxide CO2Class A - Flammable materials (limited use) Class B - Flammable liquids (limited use) Class E - Electrical equipmentDisplaces oxygen and cools the area without damaging electrical equipment
FoamClass A - Flammable materials Class B - Flammable liquids Class F – Cooking fats and oils (limited use)Smothers the fire and creates a barrier to prevent re-ignition
Wet ChemicalClass A - Flammable materials Class F – Cooking fats and oilsForms a soap-like layer that cools and smothers the fire, preventing re-ignition

Below are additional guidelines for responding to fire:

Only use a fire extinguisher if:

The fire is small and contained

You know how to use the extinguisher correctly

Your exit route is clear and you have a way to escape

Do NOT use water-based extinguishers on electrical or oil fires.

If you cannot extinguish the fire after 30 seconds, evacuate immediately.

  • In an emergency, prioritise immediate danger control and life safety. Where safe to do so, de-energise equipment and use LOTO as part of the emergency response — or immediately after to secure the area.

Follow the handling guidelines outlined in the Safety Data Sheet (SDS) for fires involving chemical spills.

Special evacuation procedures may apply if working in confined areas like electrical vaults or ducts.
