title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 10 min

The DRSABCD action plan for giving first aid.

title: 2.2.3 First Aid
body:
Workplace accidents, like electric shocks, burns, falls and cuts, can happen while working with electrical equipment. Knowing how to provide first aid helps in managing these situations. First aid is the immediate care given to a person who has been injured or becomes ill before professional medical help arrives. It aims to:

The primary priorities when providing first aid are:

Safety – Ensure the scene is safe for both the casualty and the first aider.

Consciousness – Check the casualty's response by speaking to them and assessing their alertness.

Airway – Ensure the casualty’s airway is clear and open.

Breathing – Check if the casualty is breathing.

Circulation – Check for a pulse or signs of circulation.

Bleeding and injuries – Control bleeding and manage any visible injuries.

Under the WHS Act, workers have a legal responsibility to work safely and protect themselves, their colleagues and others from harm. They are required to follow safety procedures and take reasonable steps to prevent accidents, injuries and damage while working with electrical systems.

In high-risk electrotechnology environments like construction sites, substations and industrial plants, having trained first aiders on site is a requirement under WHS regulations.

In high-risk workplaces, at least one trained first aider is required for every 25 employees.

As an electrician, you may need to provide first aid if a co-worker suffers an electric shock, a burn or a fall during work.

First aiders are expected to act in good faith and to the best of their ability when providing care in an emergency.

A first aider in the workplace is responsible for:

When administering first aid, workers may encounter a range of legal and ethical issues. Legal issues involve what is required or prohibited by law. These relate to duties under WHS legislation, regulations and other legal requirements. Failing to follow these rules may result in fines, prosecution or legal liability.
The following are examples of these issues:

Legal issueExampleImpact on the management of care
Duty of careA first aider sees an injured coworker but fails to help.May delay emergency response and result in legal consequences for negligence
Electrical safety complianceA worker gives first aid without checking if the power was isolated.May expose the responder to electrical harm; could breach WHS laws
Outside scope of trainingAn electrician gives pain medication without being trained or authorised.Can result in harm to the injured person and legal liability for the worker
Failure to report an incidentA worker does not report a serious electric shock to a supervisor or regulator.Prevents proper investigation; may lead to regulatory penalties

Ethical issues relate to doing what is morally right. These involve values like respect, empathy, fairness and professionalism. While failing to meet ethical standards might not be illegal, it can damage team trust, workplace culture and a worker’s professional reputation.

Ethical issueExampleImpact on the management of care
Lack of compassion or empathyA worker avoids helping because they are afraid of doing it wrong.Injured person feels abandoned; may lose trust in coworkers
Privacy and dignityA worker provides first aid in full view of others without consent.Can cause emotional distress and embarrassment for the injured person
Cultural insensitivityA first aider ignores a worker’s cultural preferences for touch or treatment.May cause confusion, refusal of care or discomfort
Inaction due to fear or uncertaintyA worker hesitates in a morally unclear situation.Delays in care; reduced safety culture and increased personal stress

Primary Survey

First aid procedures follow a clear process to help injured workers quickly and effectively. This includes a primary survey to check for immediate dangers and a secondary survey to look for other injuries. Managing casualties properly and using the right first aid techniques help keep the injured person stable until medical help arrives.

The common method for conducting a primary survey is DRSABCD. DRSABCD stands for: Danger, Response, Send for help, Airway, Breathing, CPR and Defibrillation. It is a step-by-step approach that helps you assess an emergency situation and take immediate action. It ensures that critical first aid steps are followed in the correct order. The following are the steps for implementing DRSABCD:

• Danger – Before approaching the casualty, make sure the area is safe. You must:
  • Check for live wires, exposed circuits and other electrical hazards.
  • If possible, isolate the power source using LOTO procedures.
  • Wear the correct PPE, such as insulated gloves and boots.
  • Response – Check if the person is conscious and responsive. You can:
  • Ask simple questions like, ‘Are you okay?’
  • Gently tap their shoulder to check for a response.
  • Send for Help – Call for emergency assistance or notify a supervisor. Provide details about the accident, including the type of injury and the location.
  • If possible, instruct a colleague or bystander to call for help while you provide care.
  • Airway – Check if the person’s airway is clear.
  • If they are unconscious, tilt their head back and lift their chin to open the airway.
  • Remove any obstructions, such as vomit or loose objects.
  • Breathing – Check if the person is breathing normally. Look for chest movement and listen for breath sounds.

If they are not breathing, start CPR immediately.

  • CPR (Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation) – If the person is not breathing, perform CPR. You must:
  • Give 30 chest compressions followed by 2 rescue breaths.
  • Continue until help arrives or the person starts breathing.
  • Defibrillation – If an Automated External Defibrillator (AED) is available, use it.
  • Turn on the AED and follow the audio instructions.
  • Ensure the area is clear before delivering a shock.
  • Note: If more than one person is injured, always attend to the unresponsive person first. Prioritise care based on the severity of injuries and the immediate risk to life. For example, if two workers are injured — one suffering from electric shock and another with a minor cut — you should focus on the electric shock victim first.

title: The video below demonstrates DRSABCD: Health Partners DRSABCD Delivery
displayTitle: The video below demonstrates DRSABCD: Health Partners DRSABCD Delivery

body:
Secondary Survey

After completing the primary survey (DRSABCD), you can perform a more detailed secondary survey to assess the casualty's condition and identify any injuries that need attention.

The secondary survey involves two key steps: asking questions to assess casualty’s condition and checking for injuries.
Ask questions to assess casualty’s condition. Use the SAMPLE method to gather information about the casualty’s condition:

SampleDescription
Signs and symptomsAsk the casualty how they feel and look for signs like swelling, bleeding, bruising or deformities. Symptoms may include pain, numbness, dizziness or trouble breathing. Helps determine the severity of the injury and the type of care needed
AllergiesAsk if the casualty has any known allergies to medication, food or environmental triggers (e.g. insect stings, latex). Helps avoid allergic reactions when administering first aid or when medical personnel provide further treatment
MedicationsAsk if the casualty is taking any prescribed or over-the-counter medication (e.g. asthma inhalers, heart medication) and any recent painkillers or supplements. Knowing this helps avoid adverse reactions or complications during treatment
Past medical historyAsk about any previous injuries, surgeries or medical conditions (e.g. heart problems, diabetes, epilepsy). Determines if the current condition is related to an existing medical issue and informs the type of care needed
Last mealAsk when they last ate or drank. Important if the casualty may need surgery or medication later, as an empty or full stomach could affect treatment options
Events leading up to the incidentAsk what happened just before the injury (e.g. electric shock, fall, equipment malfunction). Helps identify the cause of the incident and any contributing factors to prevent further injuries
• Check for Injuries. Carefully examine the casualty from head to toe to identify visible injuries or other issues:

Body PartActions to Take
HeadGently feel the head and scalp for cuts, bumps or swelling. Check for any bleeding.
EyesOpen the casualty’s eyelids gently. Check if the pupils are equal in size and responsive to light. Look for any debris or foreign objects.
Neck and spineGently feel the neck for swelling or deformities. If you suspect a spinal injury, do not move the casualty — stabilise their head and neck until help arrives.
Chest and abdomenPlace your hands on the chest and observe if it rises evenly. Gently press the abdomen to check for tenderness, swelling or rigidity. Look for signs of bruising or shallow breathing.
Arms and legsSqueeze the arms and legs gently from top to bottom. Look for deformities, swelling or bleeding. Ask the casualty if they can feel you touching them and if they can move their fingers and toes.
SkinFeel the skin’s temperature. If it is cold, pale or clammy, it may indicate shock. Look for burns, cuts or signs of sweating.
BackIf there is no suspected spinal injury, roll the casualty carefully onto their side and check for bleeding, bruising or swelling along the back and spine. Use the recovery position if needed.

After examining the casualty:

Provide care based on your training and within your limits.

Keep the casualty calm and still while waiting for medical help.

Report findings to the supervisor or other relevant persons (e.g. safety officer).

If the casualty’s condition changes, update emergency responders when they arrive.

_graphic.alt: Note: If more than one person is injured, always attend to the unrespo

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 12 min

Clearing an airway, rescue breaths and performing CPR.

title: 2.2.4 CPR
body:
Providing first aid can save lives, especially when a person’s breathing or circulation is affected. If the casualty has an airway obstruction, the focus should be on clearing the blockage using back blows or abdominal thrusts. However, if they become unresponsive and stop breathing, start CPR immediately to restore circulation and supply oxygen to the brain. Knowing how to respond to airway obstruction, respiratory arrest and cardiopulmonary arrest is essential for providing life-saving care until medical help arrives.

Airway Obstruction

An airway obstruction happens when something blocks a person’s airway, stopping them from breathing properly. This is a serious emergency because the brain can only survive for a few minutes without oxygen. If the airway is not cleared quickly, the person can lose consciousness and go into cardiopulmonary arrest.

In electrical work, airway obstruction can happen due to:

The following are ways to manage an airway obstruction:

  • Encourage coughing. If the person is conscious and can breathe, encourage them to cough to clear the blockage.
  • Stay close and monitor them — if they can cough, the airway is only partially blocked.
  • Do NOT slap their back while they are still coughing — this could make the blockage worse.
  • Back blows. If the person cannot clear the blockage by coughing:

_graphic.alt: Stand behind the person and place your arms around their chest.

body:
Stand behind them.

Lean them slightly forward.

Give up to 5 firm back blows between their shoulder blades using the heel of your hand.

Check after each blow to see if the object is dislodged. If the blockage clears, stop. If not, move to chest thrusts.

  • Chest thrusts. If back blows do not work:

_graphic.alt: If the person loses consciousness:

body:
Stand behind the person and place your arms around their chest.

Place your fist in the centre of their chest (lower half of the breastbone).

Grasp your fist with the other hand.

Give up to 5 sharp thrusts inward and slightly upward.

Check after each thrust to see if the object is removed. Continue alternating 5 back blows and 5 chest thrusts until the object is cleared or the person becomes unconscious.

  • If the person loses consciousness:

_graphic.alt: If you cannot see the object or the airway is still blocked, start CPR

body:
Lower them gently to the ground.

Open the airway using the head tilt/chin lift technique:

Place one hand on the forehead and tilt the head back.

Lift the chin with two fingers.

Look inside the mouth:

If you see the object, try to remove it with a finger sweep (ONLY if visible).

_graphic.alt: Start rescue breaths.

body: If you cannot see the object or the airway is still blocked, start CPR immediately.

title: Further reading
body: The link below provides guidelines on managing airway obstruction: Guideline 4 – Airway https://www.anzcor.org/home/basic-life-support/guideline-4-airway/

Respiratory Arrest

Respiratory arrest occurs when a person stops breathing, but their heart is still beating. This means that oxygen is no longer entering the lungs and circulating through the bloodstream. Without oxygen, brain damage can occur within 4–6 minutes. If untreated, respiratory arrest will quickly lead to cardiac arrest — where both breathing and the heartbeat stop.

Common causes of respiratory arrest in electrical work include:

_graphic.alt: If the person loses their pulse, immediately transition to full CPR (s

body:
• Electric shock – High-voltage shock can paralyse the muscles involved in breathing.
  • Chemical inhalation – Breathing in toxic fumes or solvents can irritate the airway and restrict breathing.
  • Smoke inhalation – Exposure to smoke from an electrical fire can damage lung tissue and stop breathing.
  • Trauma – Chest injuries from a fall or being struck by equipment can make it difficult for the lungs to expand.
  • Allergic reaction – Contact with certain chemicals or materials may cause swelling in the throat, blocking the airway.

The following are signs that a person is in respiratory arrest:

If the person is unresponsive and not breathing, but you detect a pulse, follow these steps:

  • Open the airway.
  • Use the head tilt/chin lift technique:
  • If a spinal injury is suspected, use the jaw thrust technique instead.
  • Start rescue breaths.
  • Give one breath every 5–6 seconds (approximately 10–12 breaths per minute).
  • Ensure the chest rises with each breath — if not, reposition the head and try again.
  • Continue rescue breathing until the person starts breathing on their own or emergency help arrives.
  • Monitor pulse and breathing.
  • Regularly check for breathing and pulse.
  • If the person loses their pulse, immediately transition to full CPR (see next section).

_graphic.alt: If another trained person is available, a two-person CPR can be implem

title: Further reading
body: The link below provides guidelines on casualties breathing abnormally: Guideline 5 – Breathing https://www.anzcor.org/home/basic-life-support/guideline-5-breathing/

Cardiopulmonary Arrest

If respiratory arrest is not treated quickly, it will lead to cardiopulmonary arrest. Cardiopulmonary arrest occurs when a person’s heart stops beating and they are no longer breathing. When this happens, oxygenated blood stops circulating through the body, which can lead to brain damage or death within minutes. Immediate action is required to restore circulation and oxygen flow.

In an electrical work setting, common causes of cardiopulmonary arrest include:

• Electric shock – High-voltage electric shock can cause the heart to stop (asystole) or lead to an irregular heart rhythm (ventricular fibrillation).
  • Severe trauma – Falls from heights or being struck by equipment can lead to massive internal bleeding and cardiac arrest.
  • Burns from arc flash – Severe burns can cause the body to go into shock, leading to cardiac failure.
  • Chemical exposure – Inhaling toxic substances can affect heart function and stop breathing.
  • Underlying medical condition – Conditions like heart disease or arrhythmias can be triggered by workplace stress or injury.

_graphic.alt: A qualified medical professional declares the person deceased.

body:
The following are signs of cardiopulmonary arrest:

If the person is not breathing and has no pulse, start CPR immediately. You can do a single-person CPR. To do this, you must:

  • Start chest compressions.
  • Place the heel of one hand in the centre of the chest (on the sternum).
  • Place the other hand on top and interlock the fingers.
  • Push down hard and fast — about 5–6 cm deep at a rate of 100–120 compressions per minute.
  • Allow the chest to fully recoil between compressions.
  • Give rescue breaths. After 30 compressions, give 2 rescue breaths:
  • Tilt the head back using the head tilt/chin lift technique.
  • Pinch the nose shut and give a full breath.
  • Watch for chest rise.
  • If the chest does not rise, reposition the head and try again.
  • Repeat until help arrives. Continue the cycle of 30 compressions and 2 breaths until the person starts breathing or emergency services arrive.

If another trained person is available, a two-person CPR can be implemented. A two-person CPR improves the quality and effectiveness of chest compressions and rescue breaths, which increases the casualty’s chances of survival.

To implement this, follow these guidelines:

Person 1 delivers chest compressions at a rate of 100–120 per minute.

_graphic.alt: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Plse2FOkV4Q

body:
Person 2 gives two rescue breaths after every 30 compressions.

The two persons switch roles every 2 minutes to avoid fatigue and maintain effective compressions.

There should be minimal interruption between compressions and breaths.

CPR must be continuous. Only stop if:

  • The person starts breathing and their pulse returns.
  • Emergency responders arrive and take over.
  • An Automated External Defibrillator (AED) becomes available and is ready to use.
  • You are too exhausted to continue and no one else can take over.
  • A qualified medical professional declares the person deceased.

_graphic.alt: Case Study: Simulated Medical Emergency

title: The video below shows how to provide CPR: How to perform CPR - A Step-by-Step Guide
displayTitle: The video below shows how to provide CPR: How to perform CPR - A Step-by-Step Guide

title: Further reading
body: The link below provides guidelines on CPR: Guideline 8 – Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation (CPR) https://www.anzcor.org/home/basic-life-support/guideline-8-cardiopulmonary-resuscitation-cpr/
The table below summarises the discussed conditions and appropriate immediate response:

ConditionImmediate responseImmediate responseWhen to start CPR
Airway obstructionTry to remove the obstruction with back blows and abdominal thrusts.Start CPR if the person becomes unconscious and is not breathing.Start CPR if the person becomes unconscious and is not breathing.
Respiratory arrestGive rescue breaths to restore breathing.Start CPR if the person’s pulse disappears or breathing does not resume.Start CPR if the person’s pulse disappears or breathing does not resume.
Cardiopulmonary arrestStart CPR immediately (compressions + breaths).Always — this is a life-threatening emergency.Always — this is a life-threatening emergency.

Simulations are used to help workers practise responding to emergencies involving these conditions in a controlled environment. This training helps workers to:

  • Practise assessing and managing breathing and circulation issues.
  • Improve their ability to recognise the signs of airway obstruction, respiratory arrest and cardiopulmonary arrest.
  • Develop confidence in using first aid techniques, including back blows, chest thrusts and CPR.
  • Gain experience using first aid equipment like AEDs.
  • Learn to remain calm and follow safety procedures under pressure.

Simulated training typically involves:

  • Resources – Simulations use realistic first aid equipment to support hands-on learning. This may include items such as AEDs and PPE.
  • Manikins – These are used to simulate the casualty with health conditions. This allows learners to practise CPR and other life-saving techniques.
  • Role-Play – Team members may act as casualties to simulate real-life emergencies, such as electric shocks or falls.
  • Environmental factors – Simulations may include noise, confined spaces or poor visibility to increase realism.
  • Feedback – Some manikins provide real-time feedback on CPR compression depth and rate, helping improve technique.
  • Trainers and observers – A trainer or supervisor may observe the simulation and provide feedback afterward.
Below is a simulated scenario demonstrating how the emergency response techniques discussed are applied in practice:

title: Case study
body: : Simulated Medical Emergency In a simulated training session, electricians practise responding to a medical emergency involving airway obstruction, respiratory arrest and cardiopulmonary arrest. The simulation is led by an instructor and involves a manikin and first aid equipment such as an AED. Airway obstruction: The instructor announces that the casualty is choking. The trainee encourages coughing. When the instructor states that the blockage remains, the trainee gives five back blows. If unsuccessful, they apply five chest thrusts until the instructor confirms the airway is clear. Respiratory arrest: The instructor announces that the casualty has stopped breathing but still has a pulse. The trainee opens the airway using the head tilt/chin lift and gives one rescue breath every 5 to 6 seconds, monitoring breathing and pulse. Cardiopulmonary arrest: The instructor announces that the casualty’s pulse has stopped. The trainee starts CPR (30 compressions and two breaths). When an AED arrives, the trainee follows the prompts and continues CPR until the instructor confirms the casualty is stable or ends the simulation.

Note that simulations differ from real situations in the following ways:

In a real emergency, the urgency and stress levels are higher, which can affect decision-making and reaction time.

Real-life cases may involve multiple injuries, environmental hazards or unpredictable factors that are difficult to replicate in a simulation.

In a simulation, equipment like AEDs and first aid kits is controlled and readily available, but in real situations, they may be damaged, missing or hard to access.

In a real emergency, communication may be more challenging due to noise, confusion or the absence of trained personnel.

In a real case, the emotional impact of seeing another person or a co-worker injured could affect decision-making.

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 6 min

Assessing and monitoring a casualty’s level of consciousness.

title: 2.2.5 Level of Consciousness
body:
Level of consciousness (LOC) refers to how awake, alert and responsive a person is. A person with normal LOC is fully aware and able to respond, while decreased LOC means they may be confused, drowsy or unresponsive.

An altered level of consciousness (ALOC) is any change in a person’s awareness or ability to respond. For electricians, this can be caused by electric shock, head injury, exposure to hazardous chemicals or medical issues like low blood sugar or stroke. ALOC is serious because it can affect breathing, communication and the ability to react to danger.

The AVPU scale is a quick and practical tool used to check how alert or responsive a person is. It is commonly used in first aid and emergency care across Australia, including in workplaces and by trained responders.

The scale includes four levels: Alert, Verbal, Pain and Unresponsive. If a person scores below ‘A’ for Alert — meaning they only respond to voice, pain or do not respond at all — this shows a change in their normal level of awareness. Scoring below ‘A’ means the person has an ALOC (not fully awake and aware of their surroundings), which may be a sign of a serious medical issue and could require urgent medical attention:

AVPUSigns (what you can see)Symptoms (what the person may report)
Alert The person is fully awake and aware of their surroundings.Fully awake and aware Able to answer questions and follow instructionsMay say they feel normal or slightly dizzy/tired
Verbal The person responds to verbal stimuli but may seem confused or drowsy.Slow or delayed response to questions May appear confused or disoriented Difficulty following instructionsMay say they feel confused, dizzy, nauseous or have a headache

AVPUSigns (what you can see)Symptoms (what the person may report)
Pain The person responds only to pain (e.g. pinching or pressing on the nail bed).No response to voice but responds to pain (e.g. wincing or groaning) Muscle stiffness or twitching Slow or irregular breathingCannot report symptoms at this level May be experiencing confusion, headache or severe discomfort but is unable to communicate it
Unresponsive The person does not respond to any stimuli.No movement or reaction to stimuli Pale or bluish skin (sign of poor circulation or breathing difficulty) Slow or absent breathing Loss of bladder or bowel controlCannot report symptoms at this level May be unconscious due to trauma, low oxygen, stroke or another life-threatening condition

To assess using the AVPU scale, follow these procedures:

  • Alert – Start by asking simple questions like ‘What’s your name?’ or ‘Do you know where you are?’ If they answer clearly and correctly, they are alert and fully aware of their surroundings.
  • Verbal – Speak to the person normally, then louder if needed. Ask them to follow simple instructions like ‘Open your eyes’ or ‘Move your hand.’ If they respond slowly or seem confused, their awareness is reduced, which could mean mild impairment.
  • Pain – If they do not respond to your voice, apply a firm but gentle pain stimulus, like pinching the nail bed or pressing on the shoulder muscle. If they react by moving, groaning or wincing, their consciousness is impaired, which could signal a serious problem like a neurological or circulatory issue.
  • Unresponsive – If the person does not respond to sound or pain, they are unconscious. This is a serious emergency that needs immediate medical attention.

To help workers respond to casualties with an ALOC, simulation is used to practise assessing and managing impaired awareness or responsiveness. The simulation may involve:

Manikins – Used to simulate different levels of consciousness and physical conditions (e.g. breathing difficulties, lack of pulse)

Role-play with a real person – A person acting as a casualty to simulate realistic responses

Equipment – May include PPE, first aid kits and AEDs

The goal is to develop the ability to recognise signs of ALOC and provide effective first aid based on the casualty’s condition and workplace procedures.

To manage a casualty with an ALOC:
• Conduct a primary survey using DRSABCD. You must:

Check for immediate dangers.

Assess consciousness using the AVPU scale.

Call for help immediately.

Check their airway and breathing.

Start CPR if needed.

Use an AED if available
• Conduct a secondary survey. Once the casualty is stable and immediate threats have been addressed, perform a secondary survey to identify other injuries or issues:

Use the SAMPLE method to ask about symptoms, allergies, medications and medical history.

Check the casualty's head, chest, abdomen and limbs for signs of injury (e.g. bleeding, bruising, deformities).

  • Monitor and provide ongoing care.

Continue to monitor the casualty's condition.

Keep them comfortable and reassure them until medical help arrives.

If the casualty’s condition worsens, be prepared to provide further first aid.
• Report and document.

Report the incident to your supervisor or WHS officer.

Provide a clear handover to emergency services personnel, including details of the casualty’s condition, care provided and any changes in their condition.

Complete an incident report, detailing the casualty’s condition, care provided and response to treatment.

_graphic.alt: Complete an incident report, detailing the casualty’s condition, care

title: Further reading
body: Learn more about assessing and managing casualties with ALOC from the links below: Guideline 3 – Recognition and First Aid Management of the Unconscious Person https://www.anzcor.org/home/basic-life-support/guideline-3-recognition-and-first-aid-management-of-the-unconscious-person/ Neurological Assessment and GCS https://www.ausmed.com/learn/articles/neurological-assessment-gcs

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 4 min

Recognising and managing shock.

title: 2.2.6 Shock
body:
Shock is a life-threatening condition that occurs when the body’s circulatory system fails to deliver enough oxygen and nutrients to the tissues and organs. This causes a drop in blood pressure, which can lead to organ failure and death if not treated quickly.

Common causes of shock in electrical settings include:

  • Electric shock – Disrupts the heart’s rhythm and damages internal tissues.
  • Severe burns – Arc flashes or hot surfaces can cause burns, leading to shock from fluid loss.
  • Falls – Falling from heights can cause shock due to blood loss or internal injury.
  • Severe bleeding – Cuts or puncture wounds from sharp tools can cause hypovolemic shock.
  • Exposure to hazardous substances – Toxic chemicals can trigger anaphylactic or toxic shock.
  • Heart-related issues – Electric shock can cause arrhythmias, leading to cardiogenic shock.

Signs and Symptoms of Shock

Recognising the signs and symptoms of shock is critical for providing quick and effective first aid. Symptoms may develop rapidly or worsen over time, depending on the cause of shock.

_graphic.alt: Provide comfort and reassurance.

body:
CategorySignsSignsSymptoms
CirculatoryPale, cold and clammy skinWeak or rapid pulse Bluish or grey lips and fingertips Feeling weak or faintWeak or rapid pulse Bluish or grey lips and fingertips Feeling weak or faint
RespiratoryIncreased heart rate Rapid and shallow breathingShortness of breath Tightness in the chestShortness of breath Tightness in the chest
NeurologicalDifficulty catching breath Loss of consciousness (in severe cases)Confusion or disorientation Anxiety or restlessnessConfusion or disorientation Anxiety or restlessness
DigestiveFeeling of impending doomNausea or vomiting Abdominal crampsNausea or vomiting Abdominal cramps
SkinCool to the touchFeeling cold despite normal room temperature SweatingFeeling cold despite normal room temperature Sweating

Managing shock can also be practised in a simulated training. This helps workers build confidence and develop the skills needed to respond to workplace incidents effectively. Here is how managing shock may be practised in a simulation:

_graphic.alt: The instructor announces that Frank's condition is worsening. Chris us

title: Case study
body: : Managing Shock Frank and Chris are in a training session where they are simulating a real-life emergency. The scenario involves working on a switchboard when Frank pretends to collapse. Chris quickly assesses the situation and follows the correct response steps: Ensure the scene is safe. The instructor announces that the simulated worksite has electrical hazards. Chris stops work immediately, checks for dangers and states that power should be isolated using lockout/tagout (LOTO) procedures. Once the instructor confirms the area is safe, Chris approaches Frank. Lay the casualty down and elevate legs. The instructor states that Frank is pale and light-headed — signs of shock. Chris carefully lays Frank on his back and raises his legs about 30 cm to improve blood flow. Keep the casualty warm. Chris covers Frank with a jacket and loosens any tight clothing after the instructor announces that Frank’s skin feels cold and clammy. Monitor breathing and pulse. The instructor announces that Frank’s breathing is shallow and his pulse is weak. Chris places his ear near Frank’s mouth and checks for a pulse on his wrist. If the instructor announces Frank has stopped breathing, Chris would begin CPR immediately

Provide comfort and reassurance. Chris speaks calmly to Frank, encouraging him to stay still and reassuring him that help is on the way. Call emergency services. The instructor announces that Frank's condition is worsening. Chris uses a training phone to call emergency services, providing details about the incident, Frank’s symptoms and the first aid given.

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 6 min

You will learn about the safety requirements for working at heights, including the use of harnesses and elevated platforms.

title: Apply Safe Work Methods at Heights
body:
Working at heights is one of the most dangerous activities in the electrotechnology industry. Electricians frequently work on ladders, elevated work platforms (EWPs) and scaffolds to install, maintain and repair electrical systems. Falls from heights are a major cause of workplace injuries and fatalities, which is why strict safety measures and compliance with national guidelines are essential.

Working at heights refers to any task performed where there is a risk of falling from one level to another, which could cause injury. A work area may be classified as a height risk if:

  • Work is being done at a height of 2 metres or more.
  • There are unprotected edges or gaps where a worker could fall.
  • The work surface is unstable or slippery (e.g. wet surfaces, loose flooring).
  • There is a risk of falling into machinery, holes or other hazardous areas below.
  • Lack of proper fall protection equipment, such as guardrails, safety harnesses or anchor points.
displayTitle: Apply Safe Work Methods at Heights

body: To remove or reduce risks, workers must use safety equipment to prevent falls. These include:

EquipmentHow to use
Harnesses and fall arrest systemsEnsure the harness fits correctly and is not twisted. Attach the lanyard to an approved anchor point. Keep slack to a minimum to reduce fall distance. Regularly inspect the harness and lanyard for signs of wear or damage.
Guardrails and barriersUse guardrails or temporary barriers around open edges. Ensure barriers are firmly secured to prevent displacement. Keep walkways and access points clear of obstacles.
Safety netsPosition nets below the work area to reduce the impact of a fall. Ensure the net is securely fixed to the structure. Regularly check the net for tears or damage.
Ladders and scaffoldingUse ladders that meet Australian safety standards. Ensure the ladder is placed on a stable, non-slip surface. Secure the ladder at the top and bottom to prevent movement. Inspect scaffolding for stability and secure all components before use.
Personal fall protection equipmentWear non-slip footwear to improve grip. Use safety helmets to protect from head injuries in case of a fall. Wear gloves to maintain a secure grip while working at heights.
Elevating work platforms (EWPs)Ensure EWPs are inspected and approved for use. Attach the harness to the platform’s anchor point. Keep within the platform’s weight limit and avoid leaning over the edge.

Aside from using equipment, workers must also follow these safe work practices:

Conduct a risk assessment before starting work at height.

Avoid working at heights in bad weather or low visibility.

Post clear warning signs around high-risk areas and fragile surfaces.

Use designated access points for entering and exiting elevated areas.

Work in pairs or small teams when working at heights.

Follow manufacturer load limits for ladders, platforms and scaffolds.

Ensure one worker stays on the ground to assist in an emergency.

Keep rescue equipment accessible and ready to use.
Ensure a communication system is available to call for help.

title: Further reading
body: Below is additional information on working at heights: Working at heights https://www.safeworkaustralia.gov.au/safety-topic/hazards/working-heights

Ladders

Different types of ladders are suited for specific electrical work environments:

_graphic.alt: Ensure a communication system is available to call for help.

body:
Type of ladderDescriptionWhen to use
Step ladderA self-supporting ladder with a fixed heightIdeal for indoor electrical work at low to moderate heights (e.g. installing lights or wiring)
Extension ladderA two-section ladder that extends to greater heightsSuitable for outdoor work (e.g. fixing outdoor lighting, connecting cables)
Platform ladderA step ladder with a platform at the top for stabilityBest for tasks requiring both hands, such as panel installation
Fibreglass ladderNon-conductive and resistant to electrical shockEssential for electrical work near live circuits
Multi-position ladderAdjustable ladder that can be used as a step, extension or scaffold ladderSuitable for complex electrical installations in both indoor and outdoor settings

Performing a safety check before using a ladder ensures that it is stable and safe to use. You must:

Check the condition of the ladder.

Look for cracks, bends or corrosion.

Inspect steps and rails for damage or looseness.

Ensure locking mechanisms are intact and functional.

Inspect feet and base.

Ensure the feet are clean and free of oil or grease.

Confirm that anti-slip pads are intact and in good condition.

Make sure the ladder sits evenly on the ground.

Check the load rating.

Confirm that the ladder is rated for the combined weight of the person and any tools.

Follow the manufacturer’s guidelines for load capacity.

Test stability.

Ensure that the ladder does not wobble or shift.

If the ladder is not stable, reposition it or secure it with braces or ties.

title: Further reading
body: The link below provides additional information and a video about safe use of ladders: Ladders https://www.safework.nsw.gov.au/hazards-a-z/ladders

Hazards and Precautions

The table below outlines the hazards of using different equipment and the appropriate precautions:

EquipmentHazardsPrecautions
LaddersFalling due to overreaching or improper balance Ladder slipping or shifting on an unstable surface Contact with overhead electrical wiresAlways maintain three-point contact. Keep your body centred between the ladder rails. Secure the ladder at the top and bottom.
Elevated Work Platforms (EWPs)Falls due to overloading or improper operation. Equipment tipping due to uneven surfaces Failure of hydraulic or mechanical systems.Inspect EWPs before use. Secure safety harnesses and anchor points. Operate the lift according to the manufacturer’s guidelines.
ScaffoldsFalls from edges without guardrails Scaffold collapse due to improper assembly Objects falling from aboveEnsure guardrails and toe boards are installed. Do not exceed the weight limit. Inspect the scaffold for defects or weaknesses before use.

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 8 min

This section explains how to lift, carry, push and pull equipment correctly to avoid strain and injury.

title: Use Safe Methods for Manual Handling Tasks
body:
Manual handling is a common part of an electrician’s work. Examples of tasks involving manual handling include

  • Moving electrical panels
  • Holding fixtures in place
  • Restraining cables during installation
  • Pushing conduit through walls
  • Pulling wiring through ceilings
  • Carrying toolkits and equipment
displayTitle: Use Safe Methods for Manual Handling Tasks

body:
While these tasks may seem straightforward, improper manual handling can lead to serious injuries, including muscle strain, back injuries and joint damage.

Manual Handling Injuries

Certain workplace conditions increase the risk of manual handling injuries. Identifying these situations allows workers to take preventive action.

SituationRisk factorsExample
Heavy liftingOverloading or handling heavy objects without helpLifting a large panel alone instead of using a team lift
Poor postureBending at the waist instead of using the legsReaching overhead to install a cable
Repetitive movementsPerforming the same task repeatedly without restScrewing multiple electrical panels in place for several hours
Awkward positioningWorking in confined spaces or awkward anglesWorking in a ceiling cavity to install wiring
Slippery or unstable surfacesCarrying objects on wet or uneven groundMoving equipment through a construction site
Insufficient spaceCrowded work areas that limit movementNavigating around other workers and equipment
Manual handling injuries can range from mild discomfort to long-term disability. Understanding the types of injuries helps workers take preventive action and seek medical attention when needed.

Type of InjuryDescriptionEffect
Muscle strainsOverstretching or tearing of muscle fibresPain, swelling, loss of strength
Back injuriesInjury to the spinal discs or muscles from improper liftingChronic back pain, reduced mobility
SprainsOverstretching or tearing of ligamentsSwelling, bruising, instability in the joint
HerniasPart of an organ or tissue pushes through a weak spot in the musclePain, swelling, risk of complications
TendonitisInflammation of a tendon from overusePain, stiffness, reduced range of motion
Cuts and bruisesInjury from sharp edges or dropped objectsBleeding, swelling, skin damage
Nerve damagePressure or stretching of nerves from poor posture or repetitive strainNumbness, tingling, loss of function
Crush injuriesCompression of body parts by heavy objectsSevere pain, tissue damage, possible amputation

Methods and Procedures

Manual handling is regulated under the Code of Practice: Hazardous Manual Tasks developed by Safe Work Australia. Following proper manual handling procedures based on the Code of Practice reduces the risk of injury and improves work efficiency. Here are the relevant methods and procedures:

  • Assess the task.

Identify the weight, shape and stability of the load.

Determine whether mechanical aids or team lifting is needed.

Check the work area for trip hazards or restricted space.
• Plan the lift or movement.

Determine the best route and clear obstacles.

Position your body to reduce strain.

Ensure that you have a firm grip on the load.
• Use the correct technique.

Lift with your legs, not your back.

Keep the load close to your body.

Avoid twisting or bending while carrying the load.

Push rather than pull if possible — pushing puts less strain on the body.

  • Use mechanical aids when possible.

_graphic.alt: Use mechanical aids when possible.

body:
Use trolleys, carts or hoists where available.

If the load is too heavy or awkward, ask for help.

Ensure that mechanical aids are in good working condition.
• Monitor and adjust.

If you feel discomfort or strain, stop and reassess.

Adjust your grip or technique if needed.

If you cannot safely lift or carry the load, seek assistance.

Below are guidelines on how to perform each type of manual handling task effectively:

  • Lifting techniques

Stand close to the load with your feet shoulder-width apart.

Bend at your knees (not your back) and grip the load firmly with both hands.

Lift the load by straightening your legs while keeping your back straight.

Keep the load close to your body to maintain control and balance.

Use a staggered stance to stay stable while lifting.

  • Lowering techniques

Stand close to the load with your feet shoulder-width apart.

Bend at the knees and lower the load slowly while keeping your back straight.

Keep the load close to your body while lowering it.

Place the load down gently — avoid dropping it.

If the load is heavy or awkward, ask for assistance.

Once lowered, ensure the load is stable before releasing your grip.
• Pushing Techniques

Stand behind the load with your feet shoulder-width apart.

Grip the load firmly and position your hands at waist height.

Push using the strength of your leg muscles, not your back.

Keep your back straight and elbows slightly bent while pushing.

Use slow, steady pressure — avoid sudden jerks.

Keep the load close to your body to stay balanced and maintain control.
• Pulling Techniques

_graphic.alt: Push using the strength of your leg muscles, not your back.

body:
Stand close to the load with your feet shoulder-width apart.

Grip the load securely with both hands.

Pull using your leg and core muscles, not your back.

Keep the load close to your body while pulling.

Use smooth, controlled movements rather than sudden tugs.

Keep your elbows slightly bent and your back straight.
• Carrying Techniques

Hold the load close to your chest with both hands.

Keep your elbows slightly bent and your back straight.

Walk slowly and steadily, keeping the load balanced.

Keep your line of sight clear to avoid tripping hazards.

Distribute the load’s weight evenly to avoid muscle strain.

Stop and rest if you feel the load becoming difficult to carry.
• Moving techniques

Hold the load close to your body at waist level.

Walk with small, steady steps to avoid losing balance.

Keep your head up and look in the direction you are moving.

Avoid twisting your body — turn using your feet.

If the load is heavy or bulky, ask for assistance or use a mechanical aid.

Set the load down carefully — avoid dropping it.
• Holding techniques

Grip the object firmly using both hands.

Keep the load close to your body at waist height.

Maintain a straight back and engage your core for stability.

Keep your elbows slightly bent to reduce muscle strain.

Shift your weight evenly between both feet to maintain balance.

If the load starts slipping, adjust your grip rather than tightening it.
• Restraining techniques

_graphic.alt: Stop and rest if you feel the load becoming difficult to carry.

body:
Secure the object firmly with both hands.

Keep your back straight and engage your core for stability.

Apply gradual pressure to maintain control — avoid sudden movements.

If using straps or clamps, ensure they are tightened securely.

Position your feet in a staggered stance to stay balanced.

Adjust your grip or stance immediately if the load shifts unexpectedly.

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 5 min

You will learn the correct procedures for safely removing a person from a live electrical source.

title: Demonstrate Removal of Electric Shock Victim
body:
Electric shock occurs when an electric current passes through the body. When a person comes into contact with an energised electrical source, the current can flow through the body, disrupting normal electrical signals in muscles, nerves and the heart. This can have the following effects on the body:

Precautions Relevant to Electric Shock

Following proper safety measures reduces the risk of electric shock in the workplace. Workers must follow workplace procedures and WHS regulations to minimise hazards. These include the following:
displayTitle: Demonstrate Removal of Electric Shock Victim

body:
Using earthing - Earthing provides a safe pathway for excess electrical current to flow into the ground. It ensures that exposed conductive parts do not become live if insulation fails.

Example: Earthing metal switchboards to prevent contact with live parts.

Handling extra-low voltage (ELV) - ELV is a voltage not exceeding 50V AC or 120V DC. ELV systems are used in control circuits and lighting to minimise the risk of shock.

Example: Using ELV systems for security lighting or intercom systems.

Installing fuses - A fuse contains a thin wire that melts when the current exceeds a safe level, breaking the circuit and stopping the flow of electricity. Fuses protect equipment and prevent overheating or fire.

Example: Installing fuses in domestic switchboards.

_graphic.alt: Installing residual current devices (RCDs) - A residual current device

body:
Using circuit breakers - Circuit breakers detect excessive current and automatically shut off the electrical supply to prevent overload and fire. Unlike fuses, circuit breakers can be reset after activation.

Example: Using circuit breakers in high-demand commercial installations.

Installing residual current devices (RCDs) - A residual current device (RCD) is a safety switch designed to detect and prevent electrical faults that could lead to electric shock, electrocution or fires. It works by monitoring the flow of electricity and automatically shutting off power if it detects an imbalance, such as current leaking to the ground through a person or faulty equipment.

RCDs come in two main types. These are:

  • Non-portable RCDs (Fixed RCDs) – Installed in switchboards to monitor entire circuits, cutting power if current imbalances are detected to prevent electric shocks and fires. They provide continuous protection to all connected outlets and appliances. For example, in a workplace, if an RCD is installed in a switchboard, it can protect all power points, lights and equipment on that circuit.
  • Portable RCDs - Small, plug-in devices that protect individual tools and equipment by cutting power when a fault is detected. Commonly used on construction sites and outdoor areas where fixed RCDs are unavailable. They protect only the device they’re connected to, so workers must use them for each task.

Common Causes of Electrical Accidents

The table below outlines some causes of electrical accidents:

_graphic.alt: Understanding the causes of electrical accidents is crucial for preven

body:
CauseDescriptionExample
Contact with live wiresDirect contact with exposed or damaged wiringTouching a damaged power cord
Equipment failureMalfunctioning electrical tools or poorly maintained equipmentA power tool with frayed insulation
Wet conditionsWater increases conductivity and raises the risk of shockUsing an extension lead in wet weather
Overloaded circuitsToo many devices on one circuit can cause overheating and failurePlugging multiple appliances into one outlet
Incorrect wiringFaulty or poor wiring increases the risk of shock and fireInstalling a circuit incorrectly or mismatched voltages
Inadequate insulationDamaged or missing insulation exposes live conductorsUsing a tool with exposed wiring
Poor lockout/tagout proceduresFailure to isolate and secure power sources during maintenanceForgetting to apply a lockout device when working on a live circuit

Understanding the causes of electrical accidents is crucial for preventing injuries and ensuring a safe work environment. In the event of an electrical accident, knowing how to respond safely is just as important as preventing it. Removing a person from a live electrical source must be done with extreme caution to avoid further injury to the victim or the rescuer.

Procedures

The following process outlines the correct procedures for removing an electric shock victim from a live electrical source:

Do NOT touch the person while they are still in contact with the electrical source — you could also receive a shock.

Turn off the power at the switchboard or disconnect the equipment, if it is safe to do so.

Even if the power is off, use a non-conductive object (e.g. wooden pole, dry cloth, plastic rod) to carefully push the person away from the source. While doing this, stand on an insulated surface (e.g. rubber mat, dry wood) to reduce the risk of electric shock.

_graphic.alt: Understanding the causes of electrical accidents is crucial for preven

body: Once the person is free from the source and it is safe, follow the DRSABCD action plan to check their condition and provide first aid.

title: Important!
body: Never use a metal object or your hands to remove a person from a live source. Ensure the area is dry to reduce the risk of electric shock. If the person is unconscious and not breathing, begin CPR and apply a defibrillator if available.

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 4 min

This section outlines the importance of keeping the worksite organised and free of hazards to prevent accidents.

title: Maintain Cleanliness in the Working Area
body:
Housekeeping in an electrical work context refers to the process of keeping the work area clean, organised and free from hazards. Proper housekeeping helps prevent accidents such as trips, slips, falls, electrical fires and equipment damage. For electricians, good housekeeping also ensures that electrical tools, materials and equipment are stored and handled correctly to avoid creating safety risks.

Employers (PCBUs) and workers have a legal obligation to maintain a clean and safe work environment. This is in line with the Work Health and Safety Act 2011, Sections 19 and 28. Under these sections:

  • PCBUs must provide and maintain a work environment that is safe and free from health risks.
  • Workers must take reasonable care of their own safety and ensure their actions do not put others at risk.
  • Both employers and workers must manage risks related to poor housekeeping.
displayTitle: Maintain Cleanliness in the Working Area

body:
Organisational Housekeeping Procedures

Each organisation will have specific housekeeping procedures. These procedures ensure that the work environment remains clean, safe and compliant with regulations. The general procedures include:

  • Clean-up procedures

_graphic.alt: Tool and equipment storage

body:
Clean up debris, dust and waste materials after completing each task.

Remove scrap materials and packaging immediately after installation work.

Clean up any spills (e.g. oil, water) immediately to prevent slips and falls.
• Tool and equipment storage

_graphic.alt: Following organisational procedures for maintaining a clean and organi

body:
Tools and equipment should be returned to storage areas after use.

Power cables and extension cords should be rolled up and stored properly to avoid trip hazards.

Damaged tools or equipment should be tagged and reported for repair or replacement.
• Cable management

_graphic.alt: Housekeeping in an electrical work context refers to the process of ke

body:
Secure cables using cable ties or floor cable covers.

Ensure that extension leads do not cross walkways.

Use cable management systems to keep wiring organised.
• Waste disposal

_graphic.alt: Speak with your trainer for additional instructions and guidance.

body:
Follow waste disposal guidelines for electrical materials and hazardous substances.

Dispose of damaged electrical components and insulation materials in accordance with environmental regulations.

Ensure recycling bins and waste containers are available and clearly marked.
• Inspection and reporting

_graphic.alt: Speak with your trainer for additional instructions and guidance.

body:
Conduct regular housekeeping inspections to identify hazards.

Report any housekeeping issues (e.g. blocked walkways, loose materials) to a supervisor immediately.

Keep records of housekeeping checks and corrective actions.

Potential Hazards in Relation to Improper Housekeeping

Poor housekeeping creates several hazards by increasing the likelihood of accidents and injuries. Common hazards in relation to improper housekeeping include:

HazardResults FromAssociated Risks
Trip hazardsTools, cables and materials left on the floor or in walkwaysWorkers tripping and falling, leading to sprains, fractures or head injuries
Slip hazardsSpills, moisture or oil left on the floorLoss of footing, causing slips and falls, potentially leading to bruises, sprains or broken bones
Fire hazardsImproperly stored flammable materials or accumulated debris near heat sources or electrical componentsIncreased risk of fire, burns or smoke inhalation
Blocked emergency exitsClutter or stored materials obstructing exits or access to fire extinguishersDelayed evacuation during emergencies, increasing the risk of injury or fatality
Falling objectsTools or materials stored on high shelves or unsecured platformsObjects falling onto workers, causing head injuries, fractures or bruising
Electric shock and short circuitsPoorly maintained or tangled cables, exposed wires or faulty equipmentElectric shock, burns or equipment failure
Overloading and overheatingOverloaded power outlets, using too many extension cords in one socketCircuit overheating, leading to equipment failure or fire
Following organisational procedures for maintaining a clean and organised workspace helps reduce these risks and ensures a safer working environment.

Emergency management procedures are structured plans and actions designed to respond to unexpected and dangerous situations in the workplace. First aid is the immediate care given to a person who has been injured or becomes ill before professional medical help arrives. Working at heights refers to any task performed where there is a risk of falling from one level to another, which could cause injury. Housekeeping in an electrical work context refers to the process of keeping the work area clean, organised and free from hazards. title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 9 min

You’ll learn how to actively participate in identifying hazards and reviewing control measures through workplace consultation.

title: Consult About Hazards and Control Measures
body:
In a previous subchapter, implementing risk control measures was discussed. The discussion focused on applying control measures in line with the hierarchy of control to minimise or eliminate risks in the workplace.

In this subchapter, the focus is on identifying hazards, implementing control measures and reviewing these in consultation with employers and colleagues. Effective consultation ensures that hazards are identified early, appropriate controls are selected and improvements are made based on feedback and workplace conditions.

Effective consultation involves open communication between workers, supervisors and employers to:
displayTitle: Consult About Hazards and Control Measures

body:
Active participation from all parties ensures that safety procedures are practical and consistently applied.

Below are some ways to contribute during consultation processes:

  • When identifying hazards

Share observations about potential hazards based on their direct work experience (e.g. faulty equipment, poor lighting).

Report near-misses and past incidents to help identify recurring risks.

Ask questions about potential risks if something seems unclear or unsafe.

Provide input on environmental factors (e.g. noise, clutter, weather conditions) that could create hazards.

Listen to others' experiences and suggest solutions based on similar situations.

  • When implementing risk controls

Provide feedback on whether proposed control measures are practical and effective.

Suggest alternative solutions if the proposed control measures seem unworkable or unsafe.

Confirm understanding of the control measures and how to apply them.

Request additional resources or training if needed to apply the controls effectively.

Identify barriers to implementing the controls and suggest ways to overcome them.

  • Reviewing Controls

_graphic.alt: This subchapter covers how to follow specific workplace instructions a

body:
Provide feedback on whether the controls are working as intended.

Report any issues or unintended consequences of the control measures.

Suggest improvements if the controls are ineffective or difficult to follow.

Discuss incidents or near-misses that occurred despite the controls being in place.

Participate in follow-up meetings to evaluate the long-term effectiveness of the controls.

Consulting can be done through both structured processes and on-the-job communication while the task is being performed. Below are some ways to do this:
• Pre-start meetings or toolbox talks

Toolbox talks are short, structured meetings held before starting work to identify potential hazards and agree on control measures.

They are led by a supervisor or team leader and allow workers to raise concerns and suggest ways to reduce risks.

Example: Before starting electrical installation work, the team holds a toolbox talk:

Identifying hazards: The team identifies the risk of electric shock, falling objects and poor lighting.

Implementing risk controls: Workers agree to wear insulated gloves, secure loose tools and use temporary lighting.

Reviewing controls: After the work is completed, the team discusses whether the lighting was sufficient and whether additional measures are needed for future jobs.
• Job Safety Analysis

_graphic.alt: Active participation from all parties ensures that safety procedures a

body:
JSAs are conducted through a structured meeting between the supervisor and workers and the findings are recorded in a JSA form or document.

Each step is analysed for potential hazards and suitable control measures are selected.

JSAs are often reviewed and updated if new hazards arise during the task.

Example: Before installing a new circuit breaker:

Identifying hazards: The team identifies risks such as electric shock, arc flash and muscle strain from lifting heavy equipment.

Implementing risk controls:

De-energise the circuit and use lockout/tagout (LOTO) procedures.

Use insulated tools and wear arc-rated PPE (personal protective equipment).

Use a team lift to position the circuit breaker safely.

Reviewing controls:

After installation, the team discusses the task and identifies that better lifting aids could reduce strain in future jobs.

The JSA document is updated to reflect this improvement.
• Safety walkthroughs and inspections

Supervisors and safety officers conduct regular site inspections to identify hazards and ensure that control measures are followed.

Workers may also provide feedback during these inspections.

Example: During a safety walkthrough, a supervisor notices exposed wiring near a high-traffic area:

_graphic.alt: Participate in follow-up meetings to evaluate the long-term effectiven

body:
Identifying hazards: Exposed live wires are identified as a potential electric shock risk.

Implementing risk controls: The supervisor arranges for immediate insulation of the wires and sets up warning signs until repairs are completed.

Reviewing controls: The team updates the site’s safety procedures to include regular checks for exposed wiring.
• Incident and near-miss reporting

After an incident or near-miss, consultation is required to identify the root cause and improve controls.

Workers and supervisors meet to discuss what happened and agree on corrective actions.

Example: After a worker trips over loose cables:

Identifying hazards: The team identifies that poor cable management is creating a tripping hazard.

Implementing risk controls: Workers secure the cables using floor covers and introduce cable hooks.

Reviewing controls: After one week, the team assesses whether the new cable management system has reduced trip hazards and makes further adjustments if needed.
• Informal on-the-job communication

Workers are encouraged to raise concerns about hazards with supervisors at any time.

This is usually done face-to-face but can also be reported via phone or radio if the supervisor is not immediately available.

Workers may also report hazards to a team leader or raise issues informally during short breaks.

_graphic.alt: The JSA document is updated to reflect this improvement.

body:
Supervisors are responsible for addressing concerns promptly and making adjustments as needed.

Example: While working on a switchboard, a worker notices that the lighting is too dim to see the wiring properly:

Identifying hazards: Poor visibility is identified as a safety risk.

Implementing risk controls: The supervisor arranges for additional lighting before work continues.

Reviewing controls: After work is completed, the team assesses whether the lighting was sufficient and recommends permanent improvements if needed.

Consultation must be done regularly. This ensures that safety is continuously monitored and improved throughout the work cycle:

• Before work – Conduct pre-start inspections, review job safety analysis (JSA) and discuss known hazards during toolbox talks or pre-shift meetings.
  • During work – Stay alert for new hazards, stop work if an immediate danger arises and adjust control measures if conditions change.
  • After work – Conduct a post-task review to assess whether the control measures were effective, report any issues and suggest improvements for future work.

Effective communication during consultation ensures that all parties understand the hazards and control measures clearly. Below are some verbal communication techniques:

  • Be clear and direct.

Use simple, direct language to describe hazards and controls.

Avoid technical terms unless everyone understands them.

Provide specific examples rather than vague statements.

Example: Instead of saying, ‘The area is unsafe,’ say, ‘There are exposed wires on the floor near the switchboard.’

  • Use active listening.

_graphic.alt: Informal on-the-job communication

body:
Maintain eye contact and show that you are listening.

Avoid interrupting while others are speaking.

Summarise or repeat key points to confirm understanding.

Example: ‘So, you’re saying the insulation on the cable is damaged and should be replaced immediately?’

  • Ask open-ended questions.

Use open-ended questions to encourage discussion and feedback.

Avoid yes/no questions that limit responses.

Example: Instead of saying, ‘Is it safe to use this tool?’ say, ‘What should I check to make sure this tool is safe to use?’
• Stay professional and respectful.

Speak calmly and maintain a professional tone.

Avoid blaming others or using aggressive language.

Respect differing opinions and focus on finding solutions.

Example: Instead of saying, ‘Why didn’t you tell me the cable was damaged?’ say, ‘Should we set up a system to report damaged cables more quickly?’

  • Focus on solutions.

_graphic.alt: Effective communication during consultation ensures that all parties u

body:
Keep the discussion focused on resolving issues rather than assigning blame.

Discuss practical solutions and agree on clear actions.

Example: Instead of saying, ‘This keeps happening,’ say, ‘What can we do differently to prevent this?’

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 7 min

This subchapter explains how to recognise hazards and report them to the appropriate person using workplace procedures.

title: Report Identified Hazards
body:
In the previous section, we discussed the importance of consulting about hazards and control measures with supervisors and team members. After consulting about these, the next step is to actively identify hazards in your work area and report them correctly to prevent accidents and maintain a safe working environment.

You have already learnt about hazards found in electrotechnology workplaces, such as asbestos, hazardous gases and confined spaces. The table below gives more examples of hazards found in specific electrical work areas, along with the risks they can cause:

Work areasPotential hazardsAssociated risks
Electrical switchboards and control panelsLive terminals and exposed wiring Faulty breakers and poor insulation Overloaded circuitsElectric shock Burns from arcing Fire from overheating or overloading
Ceiling and roof spacesHidden live wires Poor structural support Heat and poor ventilation Confined working spaceElectric shock Falls through ceilings Heat exhaustion Muscle strain from an awkward position
Floor and walkway areasLoose cables and debris Wet or oily surfaces Overhead workTripping and falling Slipping and injury Head injury from falling objects
Confined spacesPoor visibility Low oxygen levels Toxic gases Limited escape routesElectric shock Breathing difficulties Asphyxiation Difficulty escaping during emergencies
Elevated platforms and laddersUnstable platforms or ladders Lack of fall protection Overhead power linesFalls from height Injury from falling tools Electric shock
Work areas near machineryMoving parts and rotating machinery Faulty wiring Noise exposureCrush injuries Electric shock Hearing damage

Workers are expected to check for hazards before starting work and remain alert for new risks during and after work. Here are general workplace procedures for identifying hazards in the work area:

  • Pre-start inspections

Pre-start inspections help workers identify potential hazards before work begins. These inspections should be conducted at the beginning of each shift and whenever working in a new location. Below are some guidelines:

Inspect the worksite thoroughly. Walk through the area and check for hazards such as exposed wiring, loose connections and damaged tools.

Check for environmental hazards. Look for signs of asbestos, gas leaks or water damage that could create electrical hazards.

Ensure proper access and exit points. Confirm that escape routes are clear and not blocked by equipment or materials.

Inspect tools and equipment. Look for frayed cords, damaged plugs or faulty switches. Tag and remove any unsafe equipment.

Review work permits and safety instructions. Make sure you understand the task requirements and the hazards involved.
• Visual checks

Visual checks should be conducted before, during and after work to identify immediate hazards in the work area. These are quick checks that electricians can perform on the spot to identify issues as they arise. Below are some guidelines:

Look for trip and slip hazards. Check for loose cables, wet floors and scattered tools.

Scan for electrical hazards. Identify exposed wiring, overloaded circuits and faulty outlets.

Check for poor ventilation. Make sure air is circulating properly, especially in confined spaces.

Assess lighting conditions. Ensure the work area is well-lit to avoid misjudging distances or missing hazards.

Monitor for gas or chemical leaks. Look for warning signs like unusual smells or hissing sounds.
• Testing and monitoring

Testing and monitoring are essential for detecting hidden hazards. Workers are expected to use testing tools and monitoring equipment to confirm that work conditions are safe. Below are some guidelines:

Test for live currents. Use a voltage tester to confirm that circuits are de-energised before starting work.

Check for gas leaks. Use a gas detector to monitor the air quality for toxic or flammable gases.

Use thermal imaging. Detect overheating components or equipment by checking for heat patterns.

Monitor environmental conditions. Test for humidity, moisture and temperature extremes that could create electrical hazards.

Ensure monitoring equipment is calibrated. Make sure all testing tools are functioning properly and within the calibration period.
• Consultation

As previously discussed, consultation helps electricians gather information about existing hazards and control measures. This process can happen before starting work, during the task or after completing the task to identify risks, apply effective controls and suggest improvements. Regular consultation ensures that hazards are continuously monitored and managed.

After identifying a hazard, it is important to report it immediately to prevent injuries and ensure the right control measures are applied. The following are general workplace procedures:

  • Who to report to:

Your supervisor or team leader is usually the first person to report hazards to.

If the hazard is serious or unresolved, escalate it to the workplace safety officer or health and safety representative.

  • How to report:

You may need to report hazards verbally or in writing depending on the situation and workplace policy:

Verbal Reporting:

Example: ‘I noticed a loose wire in the switchboard in room 2B. It looks damaged and could cause an electric shock.’

Written Reporting:

Example: ‘Exposed live wire in switchboard panel 2B. Wire insulation appears damaged. Recommend isolating power and repairing insulation before use.’

  • What to report:

Location of the hazard.

Type of hazard (e.g. electric shock risk, gas leak, trip hazard)

Any immediate dangers to workers or equipment.

Any actions already taken to control the hazard.

If you suspect asbestos is present or disturbed during work, follow these steps:

Stop work immediately and notify your supervisor.

Avoid disturbing the material further — do not cut, drill or sand it.

Mark and isolate the area to prevent others from entering.

Report to the workplace safety officer and complete an asbestos incident report.

Follow the asbestos management plan to ensure proper removal by a licensed professional.

If you detect or suspect a gas leak or presence of hazardous gases:

Stop work immediately and notify your supervisor.

Evacuate the area if the gas is toxic or flammable.

Use a gas detector to confirm the presence of gas if safe to do so.

Report to the safety officer and provide details about the type of gas and source (if known).
Follow emergency procedures if the gas poses an immediate danger (e.g. activate alarms, shut off equipment).

_graphic.alt: Monitor for gas or chemical leaks. Look for warning signs like unusual

title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 7 min

You’ll learn how to accurately complete workplace safety records and reports in line with legal and workplace requirements.

title: Complete WHS Documentation and Incident Records
body:
In the electrotechnology industry, documentation is essential for identifying patterns in workplace hazards, improving safety practices and providing evidence of compliance with WHS laws. Proper documentation allows workers and supervisors to assess risks, apply effective controls and prevent future incidents.

Under Australian WHS laws and regulations, both employers (PCBUs) and workers have a responsibility to report hazards and incidents and ensure proper documentation is maintained. The following outlines the key legal requirements for workplace documentation:

  • Reporting hazards and incidents
displayTitle: Complete WHS Documentation and Incident Records

body:
Workers are required to report any identified hazards or incidents to their supervisor immediately.

All hazards, near misses and incidents must be documented according to workplace procedures.

Workers must follow workplace safety procedures and cooperate with their employer to maintain a safe work environment.
• Managing risks and control measures

Workers must participate in the risk management process by identifying hazards and suggesting control measures.

Employers must communicate known risks and control measures to workers through safety meetings, notices or written instructions.

Workers are expected to follow the documented safety procedures and use the recommended protective equipment.
• Specific requirements for high-risk work

Any work involving hazardous materials (e.g. asbestos) must be reported and recorded.

High-risk work must be supported by a written plan (e.g. Safe Work Method Statement) that outlines hazards and control measures.

Workers must follow the documented plan and report any new hazards or changes in work conditions.

In line with these requirements, workers are required to complete a range of documents to ensure that hazards are identified, assessed and controlled. These records provide a formal record of risks and actions taken to manage them. The following are examples of required documentation:

DocumentationDescriptionContent
Asbestos registerRecords the location and condition of asbestos-containing materials (ACMs) Use this when working in buildings constructed before 2003 or when asbestos is suspectedDate of assessment Location of asbestos Condition of asbestos Control measures (e.g. sealing, removal)
Hazard reportsIdentifies and documents hazards in the workplace Use this when a new hazard is identifiedDate and time of identification Location of the hazard Type of hazard Immediate action taken Recommended control measures
Incident reportsDocuments any workplace incident, including injuries, near-misses or equipment damage. Use this after an incident occursDate and time of the incident Description of the incident Injuries or damages reported Immediate action taken Follow-up actions
Job Safety Analysis (JSA)Identifies task-specific hazards and establishes control measures before starting work. Use this before starting any task with potential hazardsDescription of task Hazards identified Control measures Required PPE Approval (signed by supervisor)

DocumentationDescriptionContent
Permit to workAuthorises high-risk work to ensure that proper safety measures are in place. Use this before starting high-risk works such as confined space work, hot work or live electrical workDate and time of work Location of work Description of work Approval signed by supervisor Expiry date
Risk assessment formsAssesses the likelihood and severity of hazards and determines control measures. Use this before starting high-risk work or when new hazards emergeIdentified hazard Risk rating including likelihood and severity Control measures Residual risk after control
Safe Work Method Statements (SWMS)Provides a structured approach to high-risk work and outlines safety controls Required for high-risk work (e.g. working at heights, live electrical work)Description of task Hazards identified Control measures Required PPE Emergency procedures
Safety Data Sheets (SDS)Provides information on hazardous substances and safe handling instructions. Use this when working with chemicals (e.g. contact cleaners, coolants)Chemical name Health hazards First aid measures Handling precautions

To complete these documents accurately, workers must follow workplace procedures and apply effective written communication skills. Clear and accurate reporting ensures that hazards and incidents are properly recorded, tracked and addressed.

  • Be clear and accurate.

_graphic.alt: In the electrotechnology industry, documentation is essential for iden

body:
Use clear, simple language that is easy to understand.

Use specific terms to describe the hazard or incident.

Provide measurable details (e.g. voltage level, time of day, location).

Avoid vague descriptions or incomplete details.

Example: ‘Exposed live wire in switchboard, reading 240V at 10:30 AM.’

  • Complete all required fields.

Ensure all relevant fields are filled out.

Double-check dates, times and location details for accuracy.

Avoid abbreviations or technical terms unless they are commonly understood.
• Follow submission procedures.

Submit documentation to the site supervisor or safety officer as outlined in workplace procedures.

Ensure that reports are signed and dated by the responsible person.

Structure the information logically:

Start with: What happened

Follow with: Where and when it happened

Finish with: What action was taken and any recommendations
• Update records when changes occur.

If additional hazards or incidents are identified, update the report promptly.

Ensure any corrective actions are documented clearly.

If control measures are adjusted, revise the documentation and notify the supervisor.

Use simple and structured language to describe updates:

Example: ‘The insulation on the switchboard cable was repaired on 14 June 2025. Tested and confirmed operational.’

  • Store records securely.

Keep records in the site safety log or the designated storage area.

Ensure that records are organised and accessible for audits and investigations.

Label documents correctly and include relevant reference numbers.

_graphic.alt: Example: ‘The insulation on the switchboard cable was repaired on 14 J

body: Avoid clutter and ensure that files are stored systematically to prevent loss of information.

You switch off the power immediately.
