title: What you'll learn
body:
⏱ About 5 min

This subchapter covers how to follow specific workplace instructions and procedures to meet safety and legal requirements.

title: Follow Workplace Instructions
body:
Workplace instructions are detailed guidelines that outline how tasks should be performed safely and correctly. For electricians, these instructions provide step-by-step directions on how to manage hazards, operate equipment and follow safety protocols during electrical work.

Under Australian Work Health and Safety (WHS) laws, both employers (PCBUs) and workers have a legal responsibility to follow workplace instructions to maintain a safe work environment. Relevant regulatory requirements include:

WHS Act – sets out the legal requirements for maintaining a safe work environment. Key requirements related to following workplace instructions include:

Workers must follow reasonable safety instructions from their employer.

Workers must report hazards, incidents and near-misses immediately.

Workers are required to use personal protective equipment (PPE) as instructed.

If instructions are unclear, workers must seek clarification before starting work.

Workers have the right to stop work and report to the supervisor if they believe the work is unsafe.

WHS Regulations – provide specific rules for managing risks and ensuring compliance with workplace instructions, including:

_graphic.alt: Do not take shortcuts or skip steps.

body:
Safe Work Method Statements (SWMS) must be followed for high-risk work (e.g. live electrical work).

A permit-to-work is required for confined space entry, hot work and live electrical work.

Workers must complete training and follow instructions for operating electrical equipment.

Electrical circuits must be de-energised and isolated before maintenance or repair.

Lockout/tagout (LOTO) procedures must be followed when working with electrical equipment.

Safety Data Sheets (SDS) must be consulted and followed when handling hazardous substances.

Workers must follow handling, storage and disposal instructions for hazardous gases and asbestos.

Following workplace instructions is not just about meeting workplace rules — it is a legal requirement that protects workers and ensures compliance with safety standards.

To obtain workplace instructions, you can access them through the following sources:

_graphic.alt: Following workplace procedures ensures that tasks are carried out safe

body:
Pre-start meetings – Supervisors may provide specific instructions before starting a task, such as outlining the steps for isolating a circuit before maintenance.

Example: Before installing a switchboard, a supervisor explains the need to isolate power and wear insulated gloves.

Equipment manuals – Manufacturer’s guidelines are often attached to or stored near the equipment. They provide instructions on correct operation, maintenance and troubleshooting.

Example: An electrician checks the manual for a crimping tool to confirm the correct pressure setting for copper cables.

Safety notices – Warning signs and safety instructions are posted in work areas to highlight risks and control measures.

Example: A ‘Danger – High Voltage’ sign near a switchboard reminds workers to check for live current before starting work.

Supervisor or safety officer – If instructions are unclear, workers should seek clarification from the site supervisor or safety officer.

Example: An electrician consults the supervisor after discovering a damaged circuit breaker to confirm if work should proceed.

Worksite documentation – Examples of this are previously discussed, such as:

_graphic.alt: Following workplace procedures ensures that tasks are carried out safe

body:
Safe Work Method Statements (SWMS)

Job Safety Analysis (JSA)

Permit-to-Work documents

To ensure compliance with workplace instructions, workers should follow these procedures:

  • Read and understand instructions.

Carefully review the instructions provided for the task.

Seek clarification from the supervisor if any part of the instructions is unclear.

Make sure you understand the safety requirements and control measures.

_graphic.alt: Workplace instructions are detailed guidelines that outline how tasks

body: • Follow instructions step-by-step.

Complete tasks exactly as outlined in the SWMS, JSA or permit-to-work.

Use the correct tools and protective equipment as specified.

Do not take shortcuts or skip steps.
• Stop and report if conditions change.

_graphic.alt: Speak with your trainer for additional instructions and guidance.

body:
If the work environment changes or new hazards emerge, stop work immediately.

Report the issue to the supervisor and adjust the control measures if necessary.

Ensure that any updated instructions are documented and followed.
• Confirm completion and record outcome.

After completing the task, review the instructions to ensure all steps were followed.

Report any issues or difficulties encountered during the task.

Sign off on the task (if required) and notify the supervisor when work is complete.

_graphic.alt: Speak with your trainer for additional instructions and guidance.

body: Following workplace procedures ensures that tasks are carried out safely, consistently and in line with regulatory requirements. By understanding and following instructions, reporting changes or issues promptly and confirming completion, workers can reduce the risk of accidents and improve overall worksite safety.

title: Key points
body: In this chapter, we learnt: A hazard is always present, but the risk depends on how likely someone is to be exposed to it and whether safety measures are in place. Effective consultation ensures that hazards are identified early, appropriate controls are selected and improvements are made based on feedback and workplace conditions. Consultation must be done regularly. This ensures that safety is continuously monitored and improved throughout the work cycle. When reporting hazards, workers must include the location and type of the hazards, any immediate danger to workers or equipment and any actions taken to control the hazard. Examples of required WHS documentation include asbestos register, hazards reports and risk assessment forms. Workplace instructions are detailed guidelines that outline how tasks should be performed safely and correctly. These can be obtained from various sources such as pre-start meetings and worksite documents.

title: ✅ Activity 3.3 — incident report (Cascade Peak)
body: Scenario: You are an electrical technician at Cascade Peak Industrial Solutions. You are working alone in the workshop at 10:15 AM on 21 April 20xx. While preparing to test an extension cord, you notice a faint burning smell. On closer inspection, you see sparks coming from a power board and melting around one of the sockets. You switch off the power immediately.

title: Activity 3.3 — Type of incident
body: What type of incident is this? Choose one.

_feedback.correct: Correct — it’s a near miss. No one was hurt and nothing was badly damaged because you switched off the power in time.

_incorrect.final: It’s a near miss — no one was injured and nothing was badly damaged because action was taken in time.

You’ve worked through the whole Learner Guide and practised every self-check. The self-checks were just practice — they don’t count towards your qualification. Your formal assessment is completed with your trainer. It has three parts:

  • Knowledge Assessment — questions on WHS regulations, codes and safe practices.
  • Case Study — a scenario set in the Cascade Peak Industrial Solutions simulated workplace.
  • Workplace Assessment — your trainer observes you applying safe practices on real tasks.

Talk to your trainer about how and when each part happens. You can revisit any page in this course whenever you want — your progress is saved.

title: Your key points — a printable summary
body:
A recap of the Learner Guide’s own key points from each chapter — handy to print or save.

Chapter 1 — Prepare to enter an electrotechnology workplace

In this chapter, we learnt: There are different types of work area access permits that may be required based on the specific risks and tasks performed (e.g. confined space, high voltage work). Work health and safety (WHS) laws, regulations and codes of practice exist to reduce workplace risks, prevent injuries and ensure workers’ safety. Safe work methods are structured procedures and best practices that outline the correct way to perform tasks in a safe, efficient and compliant manner. Isolation means making sure that electrical or mechanical systems are separated from their power sources. Understanding the classification, labelling, storage requirements and how to interpret Safety Data Sheets (SDS) is crucial for using chemicals.

Chapter 2 — Apply safe electrotechnology working practices

In this chapter, we learnt: Risk management is a structured process used to identify, assess and control risks in the workplace. Emergency management procedures are structured plans and actions designed to respond to unexpected and dangerous situations in the workplace. First aid is the immediate care given to a person who has been injured or becomes ill before professional medical help arrives. Working at heights refers to any task performed where there is a risk of falling from one level to another, which could cause injury. Housekeeping in an electrical work context refers to the process of keeping the work area clean, organised and free from hazards.

Chapter 3 — Follow electrotechnology workplace procedures

In this chapter, we learnt: A hazard is always present, but the risk depends on how likely someone is to be exposed to it and whether safety measures are in place. Effective consultation ensures that hazards are identified early, appropriate controls are selected and improvements are made based on feedback and workplace conditions. Consultation must be done regularly. This ensures that safety is continuously monitored and improved throughout the work cycle. When reporting hazards, workers must include the location and type of the hazards, any immediate danger to workers or equipment and any actions taken to control the hazard. Examples of required WHS documentation include asbestos register, hazards reports and risk assessment forms. Workplace instructions are detailed guidelines that outline how tasks should be performed safely and correctly. These can be obtained from various sources such as pre-start meetings and worksite documents.
